## 📊 IDENTITY & PROTOCOL DIAGNOSIS

### The Trust-Transfer Problem at the Edge

A remote site has four questions it must answer cryptographically in under 10ms to grant an agent the same welcome it gives a signed-in human: **(Q1)** *Is this request actually from the principal it claims?* **(Q2)** *Is that principal a real, accountable entity, and who stands behind it?* **(Q3)** *Is the human principal currently authorizing this specific principal to act on their behalf?* **(Q4)** *Is this request fresh, untampered, and non-replayable?* Today the site has zero cryptographic signal to answer any of these; the only evidence is TLS termination, IP, and a User-Agent string — which is precisely why every anti-bot product defaults to "looks automated → challenge." The signal gap is what gets the agent blocked.

### Cryptographic Stack and Rationale

AgentGrid transmits a self-contained, **proof-carrying authorization** envelope on every outbound HTTP request, built from four converging 2026 standards:

| Layer | Standard | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Signing envelope | **RFC 9421 — HTTP Message Signatures** | Generic signature-input / signature-params / signature-value syntax; covers arbitrary components, parameters, and key binding. |
| Key identifier | **W3C DID Core + `did:key` method** | Self-certifying identifier — the public key bytes *are* the DID after Multicodec (`0xed` for Ed25519) + Multibase (`z` + Base58BTC) encoding. Verification works offline with no registry lookup for the base case. |
| Identity proof | **W3C Verifiable Credentials 2.0** | The agent's Passport is published as a VC; its `proof` is the operator's Ed25519 signature over the JCS-canonicalized credential subject. The VC contains `verificationMethod` → `publicKeyJwk` and binds the agent DID → operator DID. |
| Canonicalization | **RFC 8785 (JCS)** | Eliminates signature mismatches from JSON property-order, whitespace, Unicode, and number-formatting differences across Node, Python, Go, browser JS, and CDN edge workers. **RFC 9421 §2.5 explicitly calls out JCS as the canonicalization for HTTP signature components containing structured fields** (notably the `Signature-Input` header value). |
| Bot authentication | **draft-ietf-httpbis-message-signatures-agent (Cloudflare Web Bot Auth)** | Defines `Signature-Agent` header, `.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory` discovery, and the "trust words" / capability-claim pattern that lets a verifying site apply per-agent policy (skip CAPTCHA, allow POST, rate limit higher, etc.). |

### Why Ed25519 Specifically

Ed25519 is the only signature algorithm that simultaneously satisfies four binding constraints of this protocol: **(1)** RFC 9421 native algorithm identifier (`ed25519`); **(2)** W3C DID `did:key` multicodec encoding (0xed 0x01 prefix → compact Base58BTC, 44-byte DID); **(3)** FIPS-resistant, deterministic, side-channel-free, 32-byte private key, 64-byte signature, sub-millisecond verify on x86/ARM; **(4)** Cloudflare's `web-bot-auth` reference implementation, Akamai's bot manager, and Fastly's Next-Gen WAF all have first-class Ed25519 verification paths in production today. ECDSA-P256 fails on (4) — most WAFs only verify it via out-of-band calls; RSA-PSS fails on (3) and (4) — it is 30× larger and the WAF operators have not standardized on it for bot auth.

### The Verification Handshake (Trust Chain)

```
[Agent Process]
   │ 1. compose request
   │ 2. build canonical signature base from RFC 9421 component set
   │ 3. POST payload → JCS → sha256 → JCS-of-payload-digest component
   │ 4. serialize Signature-Input, call Vault.sign(did, sig_base)
   │ 5. emit Signature-Agent header with agent DID
   │ 6. emit (optional) Signature-Trust-Words and Mandate-Reference headers
   ▼
[Edge / WAF / Origin]
   │ 1. parse Signature-Input → derive signature base
   │ 2. extract keyid ("did:key:...")
   │ 3. fetch https://agents.yourdomain.com/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory
   │      (cached with 5-min TTL; stale-if-error up to 1h)
   │ 4. verify ed25519(sig_base, publicKey)
   │ 5. fetch agent's Passport VC from agents.yourdomain.com/agent/{did}
   │ 6. verify operator's signature over VC (operatorDid → operatorPubkey in registry)
   │ 7. check Passport.status == "active"; check Passport not in revocation list
   │ 8. check operator's signing key is bound (mitigates 16.2.2 — see §3)
   │ 9. resolve Mandate-Reference → fetch Mandate VC, verify chain
  10. evaluate WAF policy (skip CAPTCHA, allow POST, etc.)
   ▼
[2xx / 4xx response]
```

The **registry lookup is cached and async-off-the-critical-path** for static fetches; for high-traffic routes the WAF keeps the directory in memory with periodic refresh, so the per-request cost is one Ed25519 verify (≈30µs at the edge) plus a header parse — well under the 10ms budget.

---

## 🛠️ DETAILED TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION

### 2.1 The Eight Required and Optional HTTP Headers

All eight headers ride on every outbound request the agent makes. Required headers are mandatory; optional headers are added when their information is available and relevant.

| Header | RFC / Draft | Required | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Signature-Input` | RFC 9421 §4 | Yes | Lists signed components and signature parameters. |
| `Signature` | RFC 9421 §4 | Yes | Contains the base64-encoded Ed25519 signature value. |
| `Signature-Agent` | draft-ietf-httpbis-message-signatures-agent | Yes | Declares the agent's DID for direct discovery (lets the WAF verify without the full directory walk when the registry is pre-listed). |
| `Signature-Trust-Words` | draft-ietf-httpbis-message-signatures-agent (Cloudflare Web Bot Auth v1.1) | Yes | Free-form, space-delimited capability tags the site can match against policy ("`pay`", "`signup`", "`read`"). |
| `Content-Digest` | RFC 9530 | Yes (for requests with bodies) | `sha-256=base64(payload)` so the body is also bound into the signature. |
| `AgentGrid-Mandate-Ref` | AgentGrid extension (this spec) | Yes | Hash commitment to the active Mandate; sites resolving it get a Mandate VC back. |
| `AgentGrid-Operator-DID` | AgentGrid extension | Yes | The operator's DID; required to short-circuit operator lookups in registries that index by operator. |
| `AgentGrid-Passport-Ref` | AgentGrid extension | Recommended | URL of the Passport VC document; lets the site resolve and verify the operator signature in one fetch. |

### 2.2 Exact `Signature-Input` Header Format

The agent signs a **fixed component set** plus a **per-request set**. The fixed set is mandatory and the same for every request; the per-request set is added when those components are present and meaningful.

**Fixed component set (always signed):**
```
"@authority"     // the request's host (e.g., "api.acme.com:443")
"@method"        // "GET" | "POST" | "PUT" | "DELETE" | ...
"@path"          // path + query (RFC 9421 §2.2.2)
"@scheme"        // "https"
"signature-agent"// value of the Signature-Agent header itself (RFC 9421 §2.2.4 — sf-draft-04)
"content-digest" // value of the Content-Digest header (binds the body)
```

**Per-request components (added when present):**
```
"@status"        // not used on requests; reserved for response signing
"@target-uri"    // the full request URI (alternative to @authority+@path)
"@request-target"// legacy form
"authorization"  // bound when present; prevents header stripping
"cookie"         // bound when present
"agentgrid-mandate-ref"
"agentgrid-passport-ref"
```

**Example `Signature-Input` header (one signature, one key, multiple components):**

```http
Signature-Input: sig1=("@authority" "@method" "@path" "@scheme" \
                      "signature-agent" "content-digest" \
                      "agentgrid-mandate-ref" "agentgrid-operator-did"); \
                 keyid="did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK"; \
                 alg="ed25519"; \
                 created=1718284800; \
                 expires=1718284860; \
                 nonce="kZ5vP9mQ2nX7wR3jL8sT4uY6bN1cF0aH"; \
                 tag="agentgrid-bot-auth-v1"
```

**Parameter semantics (all from RFC 9421 §4.1.1.1 unless noted):**
- `sig1` — signature label. Multiple labels allowed when signing with multiple keys (e.g., agent key + operator key).
- `keyid` — the verification key identifier. **Must** be a `did:key:` DID. The fragment (if present, e.g., `#z6Mk...`) is allowed for key rotation within a DID document.
- `alg` — `ed25519` (per IANA HTTP Signature Algorithms registry, RFC 9421 §7.2.7).
- `created` — Unix seconds when the signature was minted.
- `expires` — Unix seconds when the signature is no longer valid. **Mandatory** for agent signatures; maximum 300 seconds (5 minutes) per the Web Bot Auth guidance to bound replay.
- `nonce` — 128 bits of cryptographic randomness, base64url-encoded. Per-request unique. **Mandatory.**
- `tag` — free-form application label; AgentGrid always uses `"agentgrid-bot-auth-v1"` to let WAFs fast-path known-valid signatures.

### 2.3 Exact `Signature` Header Format

The `Signature` header carries the base64-encoded Ed25519 signature value, prefixed by the label:

```http
Signature: sig1=:dGhpcyBpcyBhIHNpZ25hdHVyZQ==:
```

The leading `:` and trailing `:` are RFC 9421 §4 syntax for a base64-encoded signature value. The bytes are exactly the 64-byte Ed25519 signature over the signature base (see §2.5).

### 2.4 `Signature-Agent` Header (Cloudflare Web Bot Auth / draft-ietf-httpbis-message-signatures-agent)

```http
Signature-Agent: "did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK"
```

Per the draft, the value is a quoted string containing an `https://` URL or a `did:` URI identifying the agent. AgentGrid uses the `did:key` form to remain offline-verifiable for the base signature check. The verifier (WAF) is expected to also have a registry mapping the agent DID to a metadata record (the Passport VC) for richer policy decisions.

### 2.5 The Signature Base (What is Hashed and Signed)

The signature base is the canonical serialization of the `Signature-Input` parameters and the covered component values, as defined by RFC 9421 §2.5. Concretely (in order):

```
"@authority": api.acme.com
"@method": POST
"@path": /v1/charges
"@scheme": https
"signature-agent": "did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK"
"content-digest": sha-256=X48E9qOokqqrvdts8nOJRJN3OWDUoyWxBf7kbu9DBPE=
"agentgrid-mandate-ref": sha-256=7f8e2c1a9b3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9b0c1d2e3f4a5b6c7d8e9f
"agentgrid-operator-did": did:key:z6MkwGY3pH7YxGzVbHrpqXJGfCKjNmL8QrT4u2WsVbN9cDeF

"@signature-params": ("@authority" "@method" "@path" "@scheme" "signature-agent" \
                      "content-digest" "agentgrid-mandate-ref" "agentgrid-operator-did");\
                      keyid="did:key:...";alg="ed25519";created=1718284800;\
                      expires=1718284860;nonce="...";tag="agentgrid-bot-auth-v1"
```

The signature base is the LF-joined concatenation of the component lines, each terminated by a single `\n`. The `@signature-params` line is always the final line and is the SF (Structured Field) inner-list canonicalization of the `Signature-Input` value with the label stripped. The verifier reconstructs the base identically, calls `ed25519_verify(publicKey, base, signature)`, and accepts iff the boolean is true.

**Critical implementation note:** the body digest is computed over the *exact bytes on the wire*. If the request is gzipped, the digest covers the gzipped body. JCS-canonicalized JSON bodies have a separate digest value that must match the `Content-Digest` header **after JCS re-serialization** — this is the most common interop bug and is covered by the conformance test suite in §3.6.

### 2.6 `Content-Digest` Header (RFC 9530)

```http
Content-Digest: sha-256=X48E9qOokqqrvdts8nOJRJN3OWDUoyWxBf7kbu9DBPE=
```

For JSON request bodies, AgentGrid always uses `sha-256` and the digest is computed over the **JCS canonical form** of the body, then the canonical bytes are sent on the wire. The `Content-Digest` header is signed, so any body tampering invalidates the signature. Sites that do not understand JCS can still verify the digest matches what their reverse-proxy logs; sites that do can re-canonicalize and verify the body is semantically equivalent (preventing JSON re-ordering attacks).

### 2.7 `Signature-Trust-Words` Header (Capability Claims)

```http
Signature-Trust-Words: agentgrid pay signup read bot-v1
```

Trust words are **assertions by the operator, signed transitively by the agent's key via inclusion in the signed-component set** (they ride inside `signature-agent` or in a dedicated `trust-words` component when the WAF supports it). The site matches them against its policy table. AgentGrid uses a closed vocabulary: `read`, `write`, `pay`, `signup`, `browse`, `comms`, `deploy`, `compute`, plus version tag `bot-v1`. Sites that recognize `pay` may run a stricter velocity limit but skip CAPTCHA; sites that recognize `signup` may present a captcha-free signup form; sites that don't recognize any tag apply the conservative default (challenge).

### 2.8 `AgentGrid-Mandate-Ref` Header (Mandate Commitment)

```http
AgentGrid-Mandate-Ref: sha-256=7f8e2c1a9b3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9b0c1d2e3f4a5b6c7d8e9f
```

This is **not** the Mandate document — it is a SHA-256 commitment to the JCS-canonicalized Mandate object. The site can resolve the commitment to a Mandate VC by either: **(a)** fetching `https://agents.yourdomain.com/mandate/{sha256-hex}` and receiving the signed Mandate document, or **(b)** receiving the Mandate as a VP (Verifiable Presentation) inside an `application/agentgrid-mandate+json` body part on a separate control channel. Including the commitment (not the document) keeps headers small and makes the Mandate reference forward-secret (a Mandate can be revoked and re-issued with a new hash; old requests referencing the old hash will not resolve to active Mandates).

The `AgentGrid-Mandate-Ref` is **always in the signed component set**, so an attacker cannot strip or substitute it.

### 2.9 `AgentGrid-Passport-Ref` Header (Passport Location)

```http
AgentGrid-Passport-Ref: https://agents.yourdomain.com/agent/did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK
```

The site fetches the Passport VC, verifies the operator's signature over it, and checks:
1. The Passport's `id` matches the `keyid` in the `Signature-Input`.
2. The Passport's `status` is `active`.
3. The Passport's `operator` DID matches `AgentGrid-Operator-DID` and the operator's public key verifies the Passport's `proof`.

This is the **single check that defeats the 16.2.2 signer-binding forge**: even if an attacker re-signs a tampered Passport with their own key, the `keyid` in the request points to the agent's DID, and the `proof` on the Passport must be over that same agent's public key, by the operator, with the operator's key resolvable in the agent directory. Any substitution breaks the chain.

### 2.10 The `.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory` Schema

Hosted at `https://agents.yourdomain.com/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory`. Per draft-ietf-httpbis-message-signatures-agent and Cloudflare's Web Bot Auth spec, the document lists all keys the agent has ever published (so historical signatures remain verifiable after rotation) and the operator's keys for Passport verification.

```jsonc
{
  "directory": {
    "id": "https://agents.yourdomain.com",
    "expires": "2026-12-31T00:00:00Z",
    "operator": "Acme AgentGrid Operator Inc.",
    "contact": "mailto:security@agents.yourdomain.com"
  },
  "agents": [
    {
      "id": "did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK",
      "purpose": "agent",
      "passportRef": "https://agents.yourdomain.com/agent/did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK",
      "status": "active",
      "keyHistory": [
        {
          "keyid": "did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK",
          "pubkeyJwk": {
            "kty": "OKP",
            "crv": "Ed25519",
            "x": "11qYAYKxCrfVS_7TyWQHOg7hcvPapiMlrwIaaPcHURo"
          },
          "validFrom": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
          "validUntil": null
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "operators": [
    {
      "id": "did:key:z6MkwGY3pH7YxGzVbHrpqXJGfCKjNmL8QrT4u2WsVbN9cDeF",
      "displayName": "Acme Operator (Principal: did:web:acme.com:user:u_abc)",
      "pubkeyJwk": {
        "kty": "OKP",
        "crv": "Ed25519",
        "x": "k9J8vV3xK2nL5mP7qR8sT4uY6bN1cF0aH3wZ5eD7gI8"
      }
    }
  ],
  "trustWordsVocabulary": [
    "read", "write", "pay", "signup", "browse", "comms", "deploy", "compute", "bot-v1"
  ]
}
```

**Field semantics:**
- `directory.expires` — directory cache TTL hint. Verifiers should re-fetch after this time. AgentGrid sets this to 24h; Cloudflare's reference implementation uses 1h.
- `agents[].status` — `active` | `suspended` | `revoked`. Verifiers MUST refuse signatures from non-`active` agents.
- `agents[].keyHistory` — supports key rotation without invalidating past signatures. Verifiers walk the history newest-first and accept the first match.
- `operators[]` — the operator keys for verifying Passport proofs. Listed here so the WAF can verify the entire chain in one fetch.

### 2.11 The Passport VC (W3C Verifiable Credential 2.0)

Hosted at the URL in `AgentGrid-Passport-Ref`. The Passport is a signed VC with the following shape:

```jsonc
{
  "@context": [
    "https://www.w3.org/ns/credentials/v2",
    "https://w3id.org/security/suites/ed25519-2020/v1",
    "https://agentgrid.dev/ns/passport/v1"
  ],
  "type": ["VerifiableCredential", "AgentGridPassport"],
  "id": "https://agents.yourdomain.com/agent/did:key:z6Mk...AGENT",
  "issuer": "did:key:z6Mkw...OPERATOR",
  "issuanceDate": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
  "expirationDate": "2027-06-12T10:00:00Z",
  "credentialSubject": {
    "id": "did:key:z6Mk...AGENT",
    "operator": "did:key:z6Mkw...OPERATOR",
    "displayName": "Acme Research Agent #17",
    "humanPrincipal": "did:web:acme.com:user:u_abc",
    "trustDomain": "agent-owned",
    "publicKey": {
      "id": "did:key:z6Mk...AGENT#z6Mk...AGENT",
      "type": "Ed25519VerificationKey2020",
      "publicKeyJwk": {
        "kty": "OKP",
        "crv": "Ed25519",
        "x": "11qYAYKxCrfVS_7TyWQHOg7hcvPapiMlrwIaaPcHURo"
      }
    },
    "serviceEndpoints": {
      "signatureDirectory": "https://agents.yourdomain.com/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory",
      "auditLog": "https://agents.yourdomain.com/audit/did:key:z6Mk...AGENT",
      "mandateResolver": "https://agents.yourdomain.com/mandate/"
    },
    "status": "active"
  },
  "proof": {
    "type": "Ed25519Signature2020",
    "created": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
    "verificationMethod": "did:key:z6Mkw...OPERATOR#z6Mkw...OPERATOR",
    "proofPurpose": "assertionMethod",
    "proofValue": "z3JpbnRyeSBzaWduYXR1cmUgZ29lcyBoZXJl..."
  }
}
```

**The chain binds the keys in three places that must agree:** **(1)** `credentialSubject.id` = the `keyid` in the request's `Signature-Input`; **(2)** `credentialSubject.publicKey.publicKeyJwk.x` decodes to the same Ed25519 public key as the `keyid` (DID Multicodec/Multibase is reversible); **(3)** the `proof.verificationMethod` is the operator's DID, and the operator's public key is in the directory. **All three must match** — a substitution at any point fails the chain.

### 2.12 The Mandate VC (W3C Verifiable Credential 2.0)

Hosted at `https://agents.yourdomain.com/mandate/{sha256-hex-of-mandate}`. The Mandate is a VC signed by the operator with the same Ed25519 / JCS / RFC 8785 pattern. The site fetching the Mandate by commitment can verify it has not been tampered with since the request was issued.

```jsonc
{
  "@context": [
    "https://www.w3.org/ns/credentials/v2",
    "https://w3id.org/security/suites/ed25519-2020/v1",
    "https://agentgrid.dev/ns/mandate/v1"
  ],
  "type": ["VerifiableCredential", "AgentGridMandate"],
  "id": "https://agents.yourdomain.com/mandate/7f8e2c1a9b3d...",
  "issuer": "did:key:z6Mkw...OPERATOR",
  "issuanceDate": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
  "expirationDate": "2026-12-31T23:59:59Z",
  "credentialSubject": {
    "id": "did:key:z6Mk...AGENT",
    "issuer": "did:key:z6Mkw...OPERATOR",
    "capability": "pay",
    "scope": {
      "currency": "USD",
      "limitPerTransactionMinor": 5000,
      "limitPerPeriodMinor": 20000,
      "period": "P30D",
      "allowedCategories": ["saas", "compute", "data"],
      "deniedMerchants": ["casino", "crypto-exchange"]
    },
    "trustDomain": "agent-owned",
    "stepUpThresholdMinor": 10000,
    "delegable": true,
    "maxDelegationDepth": 1,
    "parentMandateId": null
  },
  "proof": {
    "type": "Ed25519Signature2020",
    "created": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
    "verificationMethod": "did:key:z6Mkw...OPERATOR#z6Mkw...OPERATOR",
    "proofPurpose": "assertionMethod",
    "proofValue": "..."
  }
}
```

The site does not need to enforce Mandate scope on its own — the Mandate exists to prove the *human pre-authorized* the agent to perform this action class. The site's job is to use the Mandate's existence and signature as a stronger welcome signal than the agent signature alone.

### 2.13 Complete Request Example (Wire Format)

```http
POST /v1/charges HTTP/1.1
Host: api.acme.com
User-Agent: AgentGrid/1.0 (https://agents.yourdomain.com; +https://acme.com/agents)
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Digest: sha-256=X48E9qOokqqrvdts8nOJRJN3OWDUoyWxBf7kbu9DBPE=
Signature-Agent: "did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK"
Signature-Trust-Words: agentgrid pay signup bot-v1
AgentGrid-Operator-DID: did:key:z6MkwGY3pH7YxGzVbHrpqXJGfCKjNmL8QrT4u2WsVbN9cDeF
AgentGrid-Mandate-Ref: sha-256=7f8e2c1a9b3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9b0c1d2e3f4a5b6c7d8e9f
AgentGrid-Passport-Ref: https://agents.yourdomain.com/agent/did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK
Signature-Input: sig1=("@authority" "@method" "@path" "@scheme" \
                  "signature-agent" "content-digest" \
                  "agentgrid-mandate-ref" "agentgrid-operator-did"); \
                 keyid="did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK"; \
                 alg="ed25519";created=1718284800;expires=1718284860;\
                 nonce="kZ5vP9mQ2nX7wR3jL8sT4uY6bN1cF0aH";tag="agentgrid-bot-auth-v1"
Signature: sig1=:dQw4w9WgXcQ...rest-of-64-byte-sig-base64...:

{"amount":1500,"currency":"USD","merchant":"acme-data","category":"data"}
```

### 2.14 The Full Verification Algorithm (Pseudocode / TypeScript)

```typescript
// packages/botauth/verify.ts
import { ed25519 } from '@noble/curves/ed25519';
import { canonicalize } from 'canonicalize';        // RFC 8785 JCS
import { createHash } from 'node:crypto';

interface VerifyResult {
  valid: boolean;
  reason?: string;
  agentDid?: string;
  operatorDid?: string;
  mandateHash?: string;
  policy?: 'allow' | 'challenge' | 'deny';
}

export async function verifyAgentRequest(
  req: IncomingMessage,
  bodyBytes: Buffer,
  dirCache: DirectoryCache,
  policyTable: PolicyTable
): Promise<VerifyResult> {

  // 1. Parse Signature-Input per RFC 9421 §4.1
  const sigInput = parseSignatureInput(req.headers['signature-input']);
  if (!sigInput) return { valid: false, reason: 'missing_signature_input' };

  // 2. Reject anything older than 300s (replay protection)
  const now = Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000);
  if (sigInput.expires < now) return { valid: false, reason: 'expired' };
  if (now - sigInput.created > 300) return { valid: false, reason: 'too_old' };

  // 3. Reconstruct the signature base per RFC 9421 §2.5
  const base = buildSignatureBase(req, sigInput, bodyBytes);
  if (!base) return { valid: false, reason: 'component_missing' };

  // 4. Verify the Ed25519 signature
  const sig = parseSignatureHeader(req.headers['signature']);
  const agentDid = sigInput.keyid;                       // did:key:...
  const pubkey = multicodecToRaw(extractPubkeyFromDid(agentDid));
  if (!ed25519.verify(sig.value, base, pubkey)) {
    return { valid: false, reason: 'bad_signature' };
  }

  // 5. Look up the agent in the cached directory
  const agentEntry = await dirCache.getAgent(agentDid);
  if (!agentEntry || agentEntry.status !== 'active') {
    return { valid: false, reason: 'agent_inactive' };
  }

  // 6. Mitigates §16.2.2: keyid must match one of the agent's published keys
  if (!agentEntry.keyHistory.some(k => k.keyid === agentDid)) {
    return { valid: false, reason: 'unbound_keyid' };
  }

  // 7. Fetch the Passport VC (cached) and verify the operator signature
  const passportRef = req.headers['agentgrid-passport-ref'];
  const passport = await dirCache.getPassport(passportRef);
  if (!passport) return { valid: false, reason: 'passport_unresolvable' };
  if (passport.credentialSubject.id !== agentDid) {
    return { valid: false, reason: 'passport_agent_mismatch' };   // chain break
  }
  const operatorDid = passport.issuer;
  if (req.headers['agentgrid-operator-did'] !== operatorDid) {
    return { valid: false, reason: 'operator_did_mismatch' };
  }
  const operatorPubkey = (await dirCache.getOperator(operatorDid))?.pubkeyJwk;
  if (!operatorPubkey) return { valid: false, reason: 'operator_unknown' };
  if (!verifyJcsVcProof(passport, operatorPubkey)) {
    return { valid: false, reason: 'passport_proof_invalid' };
  }

  // 8. Resolve the Mandate commitment (optional but recommended)
  let mandateHash: string | undefined;
  if (req.headers['agentgrid-mandate-ref']) {
    mandateHash = extractSha256(req.headers['agentgrid-mandate-ref']);
    const mandate = await dirCache.getMandateByHash(mandateHash);
    if (!mandate) {
      // Site policy decision: unknown mandate → challenge, don't deny
      return { valid: true, policy: 'challenge', agentDid, operatorDid };
    }
    if (mandate.credentialSubject.id !== agentDid) {
      return { valid: false, reason: 'mandate_subject_mismatch' };
    }
    if (!verifyJcsVcProof(mandate, operatorPubkey)) {
      return { valid: false, reason: 'mandate_proof_invalid' };
    }
  }

  // 9. Evaluate WAF policy from trust words + agent reputation
  const trustWords = parseTrustWords(req.headers['signature-trust-words']);
  const policy = policyTable.evaluate({
    agentDid, operatorDid, mandateHash, trustWords,
  });

  return { valid: true, agentDid, operatorDid, mandateHash, policy };
}
```

### 2.15 WAF / CDN Integration Pattern

The integration is a one-line addition to the WAF request pipeline: after TLS termination and before origin forwarding, the WAF calls `verifyAgentRequest()` and applies the returned `policy` to the request. Concrete integrations:

| Platform | Hook point | Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **Cloudflare** | Bot Management → Verified Bots list | Submit agent directory to Cloudflare's Verified Bots program; the WAF auto-skips bot challenges for matching `Signature-Agent` DIDs. Use `cf.bot_management.score` headers for telemetry. |
| **Cloudflare Workers** | `event.request` in middleware | Custom Worker that parses `Signature-Input`, calls `verifyAgentRequest`, sets `cf.agentgrid.verified=true` for downstream rules. |
| **Akamai Bot Manager** | Custom detector in Kona rule set | Inject `verifyAgentRequest` as a pre-origin detector; the `policy` result becomes a Bot Manager action (`monitor` / `deny` / `allow`). |
| **Fastly VCL / Compute@Edge** | `vcl_recv` or `recv` | Compute@Edge assembly is the cleanest path: parse, verify, set `req.http.X-AgentGrid-Policy`, downstream VCL applies it. |
| **AWS WAF + Lambda@Edge** | `viewer-request` trigger | Lambda verifies, sets `X-AgentGrid-Policy` header, WAF rule group matches on the header. |
| **Nginx / OpenResty (origin)** | `access_by_lua_block` | Lua module calls a local Python/Node service for verification; the response is cached in shared dict for 60s. |
| **Kong Gateway** | Custom plugin | Lua plugin implements verification, sets `ngx.ctx.agentgrid_verified`, `ngx.ctx.agentgrid_policy`. |

**The critical performance constraint:** verification must complete in **under 10ms p99** at the edge. Ed25519 verify is ~30µs. Directory and Passport fetches are the bottleneck — both are cached in a CDN edge KV store (Cloudflare KV, Fastly Edge Dictionary) with 5-minute TTL. The cold path is the 60-150ms directory fetch; the hot path is a single verify call.

### 2.16 Compatibility with Visa TAP, Mastercard Agent Pay, x402

The same signing envelope pattern extends to commerce signals. Sites that participate in any of these ecosystems get the AgentGrid signal for free by reusing the verification infrastructure:

- **Visa TAP** (Trusted Agent Protocol) — adds `X-Visa-Tap-Agent-DID`, `X-Visa-Tap-Mandate-Ref`, `X-Visa-Tap-Signature` headers. AgentGrid produces a Visa TAP VC alongside the standard Mandate VC; the two share the same operator signature and the same JCS canonicalization.
- **Mastercard Agent Pay / MPAS** — uses `agent_id`, `mandate_id`, `signature` in the JSON payload. AgentGrid embeds the standard Mandate commitment in the payload and the same Ed25519 signature covers it.
- **x402 (HTTP 402 Payment Required)** — when a site returns 402 with a payment challenge, the agent's signed response includes the same `Signature-Input` set plus `x402-challenge`, `x402-payment-intent`, `x402-signature`. The x402 server reuses the standard directory and Passport lookup to verify the agent before fulfilling the paid request.

The same Vault signing key and the same Passport serve all three ecosystems. Sites adopting any of the three get a free upgrade to agent identification if they verify AgentGrid-signed requests.

### 2.17 Library / Reference Implementation Map

| Component | Library | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Ed25519 sign/verify | `@noble/ed25519` v2 | Audited, constant-time, 30µs verify. |
| HTTP signature parsing | `@digitalbazaar/http-signature-header` | Implements RFC 9421 parsing. |
| JCS canonicalization | `canonicalize` (npm) | RFC 8785. Used for VC proofs and signature base. |
| W3C VC signing/verification | `@digitalbazaar/vc` | VC 2.0 + Data Integrity Ed25519 suite. |
| DID resolution | `did-resolver` + `@digitalbazaar/did-method-key` | Offline `did:key` resolution. |
| Cloudflare Worker | `web-bot-auth` (Cloudflare) | Reference WAF integration. |
| Header signing client | `@agentgrid/botauth` (this project's package) | Exposes `signRequest(req, vault, passport, mandate)`. |

---

## ⚠️ FAILURE MODES & TRUST HARDENING

### 3.1 Identity Spoofing — Substituting a Different Agent's Key

**Attack.** Attacker intercepts a signed request, replaces the `Signature-Agent` header with their own DID, and re-signs the modified header set with their own key. The site must reject the request.

**Defense.** The `keyid` in `Signature-Input` and the `signature-agent` value are **both signed components** (`signature-agent` is explicitly in the fixed component set, §2.2). Any substitution invalidates the signature. The verifier rebuilds the base from the *in-header* values and verifies — substituting a header value re-runs the signature base, and the signature no longer matches. The defense is structural, not policy-based.

### 3.2 The §16.2.2 Passport Signer-Binding Forge

**Attack.** Attacker generates a fake Passport claiming their own malicious agent DID is operated by a known operator DID. They re-sign the Passport with their own key and override the `proof.verificationMethod` to point to their own key. The site naïvely verifies the Passport proof against the declared `verificationMethod` and accepts.

**Defense (implemented in §2.14 step 7).** The site must:
1. Resolve the **operator's** public key from the directory using the Passport's `issuer` field — not from the Passport's `proof.verificationMethod`.
2. Verify the Passport proof against the directory-resolved operator key, not the in-Passport declared key.
3. Require that the operator's key in the directory is bound to a `did:web` operator (which is in turn bound to a human-verified domain via DNS + CAA + Web PKI) for the highest assurance tier.

The check in step 7 of the verifier (`passport.credentialSubject.id !== agentDid`) is the **single field that defeats the forge**: a Passport that names a different agent DID than the one in the request's `keyid` is rejected outright. The `AgentGrid-Operator-DID` header is also a signed component, so it cannot be silently substituted to match a forged Passport.

### 3.3 Signature Replay Attacks

**Attack.** Attacker captures a signed request (a $50 authorization, say) and replays it 100 times before the operator notices.

**Defense (defense-in-depth, all required).**
1. **`expires` parameter** — 5-minute maximum lifetime. Sites reject signatures with `expires` further than 300s in the future (mitigates long-replay).
2. **`nonce` parameter** — 128-bit unique per request. Sites maintain a nonce cache (Bloom filter or KV store) for the lifetime of the `expires` window; any replayed nonce is dropped. Cache is bounded: 1M nonces × 300s = ~1.2 GB RAM at the edge, fits in a single Cloudflare KV namespace.
3. **`Content-Digest` binding** — even a bit-identical replay hits a different nonce, but a tampered body also breaks the digest.
4. **Mandate commitment** — the `AgentGrid-Mandate-Ref` is a Mandate hash, and the Mandate VC has its own `expirationDate`. A revoked Mandate is removed from the resolver; replays of the old hash resolve to "unknown mandate" and trigger `challenge` policy, not `allow`.
5. **Anti-replay log** — the agent's audit ledger also records every signed request hash (sha256 of signature base); a defender can detect a site-side replay by correlating with their own logs.

### 3.4 Key Compromise — Agent Private Key Leaks

**Attack.** The agent's Ed25519 private key is exfiltrated (e.g., via a Vault vulnerability).

**Defense (four layers, last-line latency minutes).**
1. **Key never leaves the Vault.** The agent process calls `Vault.sign(did, payload)`; the bytes never enter agent memory. Compromising the agent does not compromise the key.
2. **Vault compromise** → key rotation. The agent's directory entry gets a new `keyHistory` record; the old `keyid` is marked `validFrom`-ended; new signatures use the new key. The rotation propagates within 60s via directory cache TTL.
3. **Compromised key signing** → short signature lifetime (5 min) caps the blast radius. After 5 min, no new requests sign successfully with the old key.
4. **Revocation** → operator sets `agents[].status = "revoked"`. Sites that re-fetch the directory within 5 min (Cloudflare KV push-invalidation hook) refuse all signatures from this agent within one cache TTL. Sites on longer TTLs fail closed after `expires`.

The 60-second **invalidation push** is implemented as a `Cache-Tag: agentgrid-revoke` header on the directory response; Cloudflare KV, Fastly, and Akamai all support purge-by-tag in <5s globally. Operator-triggered revocation is one operator-signed POST to `/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory/revoke?did=...`.

### 3.5 Mandate Forgery or Substitution

**Attack.** Attacker substitutes the `AgentGrid-Mandate-Ref` value with the hash of a Mandate they authored, claiming broader scope.

**Defense.**
1. **The Mandate ref is a signed component.** Substitution breaks the request signature.
2. **The Mandate is a signed VC.** Resolving the hash returns the Mandate; the verifier checks the operator's signature over the Mandate against the directory-resolved operator key.
3. **The Mandate chain check.** If the Mandate has a `parentMandateId`, the verifier walks up the chain; each parent's `credentialSubject.id` must equal the child's `issuer`, and the root Mandate's `issuer` must equal the operator's DID from the Passport. Any break in the chain is a forgery indicator → `deny`.
4. **The Mandate is bound to a specific agent.** `credentialSubject.id` (the Mandate's subject) must equal the agent DID in the request. A Mandate for a different agent cannot be used to authorize a different agent's requests.

### 3.6 WAF Adoption Gaps and the CAPTCHA Fallback

**Failure mode.** A site does not implement Web Bot Auth verification. The site still gets the `User-Agent` and the headers, but cannot make a policy decision based on the cryptographic signal.

**Fallback strategy (graceful degradation).**
1. **For sites honoring Web Bot Auth** — the agent sails through. No CAPTCHA, no challenge, no rate limit penalty.
2. **For sites with the headers but no verification code** — the User-Agent contains `AgentGrid/1.0` and a link to the agent directory. Sites that hardcode "no User-Agent match → challenge" can switch to "User-Agent match → present visible CAPTCHA" as a soft fallback. This is a one-line WAF rule change.
3. **For sites with no recognition at all** — the executor invokes the §7 STEP_UP fallback: push a notification to the human's phone with the site URL, CAPTCHA type, and an inline "solve this" button. The human solves the CAPTCHA in the agent's browser context, the agent continues. This is **one tap on a hard wall**, not a tap on every action. The MCP server tracks a per-site fallback counter and prompts only once per site per 24h.

The fallback is **explicitly not** automated CAPTCHA solving or detection evasion. That is the malicious-bot path the whole signed-agent ecosystem exists to replace; it would (a) violate every site's ToS, (b) get the agent network banned, (c) destroy the trust story, and (d) be detected within weeks and trigger permanent agent-DID revocation by Cloudflare's Verified Bots program. The acceptance posture is: "we are an accountable agent; we honor robots.txt and rate limits; if you don't have Web Bot Auth yet, we'll route one tap to the human, and we'll be back when you do."

### 3.7 Mandate-Ref Resolution Failures (Transient Network Issues)

**Failure mode.** The site cannot reach `agents.yourdomain.com/mandate/{hash}` due to network partition.

**Defense.** Sites should treat **unknown Mandate refs as `challenge`, not `deny`**. The signature and Passport chain are already verified; the Mandate is the *enhancement* that proves the human pre-authorization. The site can serve the request under a degraded trust tier:
- Signature valid + Passport valid + Mandate resolvable → `policy: allow`
- Signature valid + Passport valid + Mandate unresolvable → `policy: challenge` (or monitor-only)
- Signature valid + Passport invalid → `policy: deny`

This avoids a single network hiccup blocking the entire agent population. Sites that want strict enforcement can flip the default to `deny` via a config flag.

### 3.8 Clock Skew Breaking `created` / `expires` Validation

**Failure mode.** Agent and site clocks are off by 30s; agent's `created` is in the site's future.

**Defense.** Sites accept a **±60s leeway window** on `created` (must not be more than 60s in the future) and `expires` (must not be in the past, allowing 60s clock drift). The leeway is a per-site config; the default is 60s. Sites with strict NTP can set it to 5s; the WAF logs rejections so misconfigurations are visible. Sites with very loose clocks should set the leeway to 120s and audit the requests they accept.

### 3.9 CAEP-Style Continuous Evaluation Push (Operator → Site)

**Standard.** **CAEP / SSF** (Continuous Access Evaluation Profile / Shared Signals Framework, OpenID Foundation) defines push-based revocation signals: when an operator revokes a Passport, a signed CAEP event is pushed to subscribed relying parties in <1s.

**Implementation.** AgentGrid publishes a CAEP `verification` event type with the agent DID, signed by the operator, pushed to sites that have registered for updates. The push is over HTTPS POST to a site-registered webhook URL (listed in the directory as `caepPushEndpoint` per agent or per operator). Cloudflare and Okta both have CAEP receiver implementations. The site marks the agent as `revoked` in its local cache immediately, regardless of directory TTL.

This is the **fastest revocation path** in the system: sub-second from operator click to site enforcement, vs. the 60s directory cache TTL for sites that do not implement CAEP.

### 3.10 Rate Limiting as a Backstop

Independent of Mandate limits (which are per-Mandate, not per-site), every signed request is rate-limited by the WAF using the agent DID as the bucket key:

- **Per-agent global cap** — 1000 requests/minute across all sites.
- **Per-agent per-site cap** — 100 requests/minute, default. Sites can configure higher for trusted agents.
- **Per-mandate per-site cap** — the Mandate's `limitPerPeriod` divided by the operator's expected request rate, with a floor of 50/minute.

Rate-limit responses are 429 with `Retry-After`. Rate-limited requests still produce audit ledger entries (the policy decision is logged). Sites that do not implement per-agent rate limiting can rely on the network-layer backstop: Cloudflare's global rate limit rule keyed on `Signature-Agent` header value.

### 3.11 Trust-Work Spoofing (Operator Mis-claiming Capabilities)

**Attack.** A malicious operator includes `pay` in `Signature-Trust-Words` for an agent that has no payment Mandate, hoping the site will extend credit.

**Defense.** The `Signature-Trust-Words` are advisory, not authoritative. The site's policy must correlate trust words against the resolved Mandate: if the agent claims `pay` but the Mandate's `capability` is `browse`, the site must down-rank to `challenge`. The site should also weight trust words by the operator's reputation (operators with a history of consistent Mandates get higher weight; new operators get a probationary tier). The protocol is designed so trust words are an *optimization signal*, never the *authorization signal* — that lives in the Mandate VC and the operator signature.

### 3.12 Audit and Compliance Endpoints

For SOC 2 / ISO 27001 / PCI-DSS auditors, the agent directory exposes:

- `GET /.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory` — agent and operator key inventory.
- `GET /agent/{did}` — Passport VC.
- `GET /mandate/{hash}` — Mandate VC.
- `GET /revocations/{did}` — signed Certificate Revocation List (CRL) in X.509-style format, signed by the operator.
- `GET /audit/{did}?since={ts}` — paginated audit ledger view (read-only).
- `GET /transparency/{date}` — daily Merkle root of the audit ledger, signed by the operator (the external anchoring from §8).

All endpoints are CORS-restricted to known verifier domains (Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly, large e-commerce sites) to prevent passive scanning. Audit queries require the auditor to present a signed "right-to-audit" token, itself a Verifiable Presentation bound to the auditor's DID.

### 3.13 The Conformance Test Suite

To prevent implementation drift, AgentGrid publishes an open-source conformance suite (`@agentgrid/botauth-test-vectors`) with 256 test vectors covering:

- Canonical signature base construction (RFC 9421 §2.5 edge cases).
- JCS canonicalization of every header value type (string, token, sf-binary, sf-list, sf-dict).
- Cross-implementation signatures (Node, Python, Go, Rust, browser JS).
- Replay attack vectors (nonce reuse, expired timestamps, future timestamps).
- Passport forge vectors (mismatched `id`/`keyid`, mismatched operator, fake `verificationMethod`).
- Mandate chain vectors (forged parent, missing parent, broken delegation).
- HTTP field case-folding (RFC 9421 §2.1 — header field names are case-insensitive in retrieval but canonicalized to lowercase in the signature base).

WAF vendors integrating AgentGrid verification run the suite in CI; passing grants the right to display "AgentGrid-Verified" in the WAF admin UI and to be listed on the integration page.

### 3.14 Residual Risks (Honest Accounting)

The following cannot be fully eliminated by this protocol and are documented openly:

1. **Operator-level compromise.** If the operator's key is compromised, an attacker can mint Passports and Mandates at will. Defense: operator keys live on-device in the Secure Enclave (§16.6) with biometric gating; key rotation within minutes; CAEP push of revocations within 1s.
2. **Directory hosting compromise.** A malicious directory can serve attacker-controlled public keys for legitimate agent DIDs. Defense: the directory is a `did:web` site bound to a Web PKI-validated domain with CAA records; DNSSEC + CAA + HSTS pin the operator's hosting provider; the directory is replicated across 3+ geographically diverse providers (Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront, Fastly) with cross-signed consistency checks.
3. **First-request trust gap.** The site has never seen this agent before. Defense: each first request carries a Passport VC fetch; the site can also subscribe to a daily directory digest (signed by the operator) that pre-lists agents authorized in the next 24h.
4. **Malicious but authorized agent.** A perfectly verified, properly mandated agent that does something harmful but legal. Defense: this is the §9 semantic intent attack; it is *not solvable at the protocol layer* and is the human's responsibility via tight Mandates and STEP_UP on irreversible actions. The signed audit ledger makes post-hoc accountability possible; nothing makes it impossible.
5. **Side-channel on signature base.** Length-based timing attacks on the signature base construction. Defense: the construction is a constant-time string concatenation; the only data-dependent operation is the JCS canonicalization, which uses existing audited libraries.

The architecture is **honest about the trust boundaries**: the protocol proves *who* and *what was authorized*. It does not prove *the agent's reasoning was sound*. The human chooses the Mandate envelope; the system enforces it; the math proves it was honored. Anything beyond that is a societal problem, not a cryptographic one.

---

# AGENT SYSTEM 2: W3C DID METHOD:KEY & METHOD:WEB SPECIFIER

**🎯 Protocol Focus:** Specify DID-to-public-key bindings, transition pathways, and decentralized resolution mechanics for remote verifiers.

<think>
The user is asking me to execute my assigned role as a W3C DID method:key & method:web Specifier within the AgentGrid system. My target task vector is "Specify DID-to-public-key bindings, transition pathways, and decentralized resolution mechanics for remote verifiers."

The core question I need to address is: "How do I say to a random site that an agent verified by AgentGrid is safe and is being used by this user? How do I convey that to a site so that they don't block that?"

I need to provide concrete cryptographic details, header formats, registry protocols, API schemas, and WAF integration patterns.

Let me structure this with the three required sections:

1. ## 📊 IDENTITY & PROTOCOL DIAGNOSIS
2. ## 🛠️ DETAILED TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION
3. ## ⚠️ FAILURE MODES & TRUST HARDENING

The core technical mechanism that solves this is:
- RFC 9421 HTTP Message Signatures (HTTP Message Signatures for HTTP Message Authentication)
- Cloudflare Web Bot Auth
- W3C DID (did:key and did:web methods)
- Verifiable Credentials (W3C VC 2.0)
- JCS (RFC 8785) canonicalization
- A `.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory` for key publication

Let me build out the deep technical specification:

### DID Method:key
- The DID is derived from the public key itself
- Format: `did:key:z6Mk...` for Ed25519
- Multicodec: `0xed` for Ed25519 public key
- Multibase: Base58BTC (z prefix)
- Self-certifying, no infrastructure needed
- Limitation: key rotation requires DID change

### DID Method:web
- Format: `did:web:agents.yourdomain.com:agent-id`
- Resolves via HTTPS GET to `https://agents.yourdomain.com/agent-id/did.json`
- The DID document includes verification methods, authentication relationships
- Allows key rotation without changing the DID
- Supports service endpoints

### Transition pathway
- Start with did:key
- Bootstrap did:web with the same key
- did:key remains as offline-verifiable fallback
- did:web for online resolution, key rotation, service endpoints

### How a remote site verifies:
1. Agent makes HTTP request to site
2. Browser/intercepting proxy adds Signature and Signature-Input headers (RFC 9421)
3. Signed components include: @authority, @path, @query, @signature-params (with keyid, alg, created, expires, nonce, tag)
4. Site receives request, extracts `keyid` (which is the DID)
5. Site resolves DID to get public key:
   - For did:key: decode directly from the DID
   - For did:web: HTTPS GET to resolve
6. Site verifies signature with the public key
7. Site checks the key is in a trust registry (Cloudflare's agent registry, custom agent registry)
8. Site applies policy (skip CAPTCHA, log to audit, etc.)

- Domain binding to prevent replay across sites

- WAF integration patterns
