# AgentGrid — Registry Backup, Disaster Recovery & Cross-Site Agent Verification Protocol

---

## 1. ## 📊 IDENTITY & PROTOCOL DIAGNOSIS

### 1.1 The Core Verification Problem Decomposed

The question "how do I say to a random site that an agent is safe" decomposes into three cryptographically distinct assertions that the site must verify before bypassing bot walls:

| Assertion | Cryptographic Primitive | Verification Path |
|---|---|---|
| **Who is this agent?** | W3C DID `did:key:z6Mk...` derived from the agent's Ed25519 public key (Multicodec `0xed01`, Multibase Base58BTC `z`) | Self-resolving — no network call required |
| **Who is the accountable human?** | Operator binding via Passport `proof.jws` over JCS-canonicalized document, signed by the operator's distinct Ed25519 key | Online: fetch Passport from registry; offline: cached copy |
| **Is the agent currently authorized?** | Mandate chain signature + revocation status (live) | Online: registry query + `/.well-known/revocation` |
| **Is this specific request authentic?** | RFC 9421 HTTP Message Signatures with `Signature-Input` covering method, path, authority, and a fresh nonce | Online: fetch public key from `.well-known` directory; verify signature |

A site needs all four to confidently **whitelist the agent** rather than just tolerate it.

### 1.2 Why This Differs From "Bot Detection Evasion"

The **Web Bot Auth** protocol (Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly adoption throughout 2026) is the inverse of detection-evasion:

```
Traditional bot detection:  "Looks automated → CHALLENGE"
Web Bot Auth:                "Proves accountability → ALLOW"
```

The agent's *private* signing key never leaves the Vault (§4 of the Blueprint); the agent's HTTP client (the executor, never the LLM) signs every outbound request. Sites that have integrated Web Bot Auth verify the signature against a published public key, check the operator's reputation/revocation status, and apply policy. The protocol is **opt-in for sites** — not all sites honor it, so a graceful STEP_UP-to-CAPTCHA fallback is mandatory (Blueprint §6).

### 1.3 The Two-Tier Trust Architecture

```
Tier 1 — Self-certifying (offline-verifiable, no registry call):
   did:key  →  extract Ed25519 public key via Multibase/Decoding
   JCS(Passport)  →  verify operator's signature with the agent's pubkey
   JCS(Mandate)   →  verify issuer's signature with the issuer's pubkey

Tier 2 — Registry-dependent (requires online lookup):
   Operator binding     →  confirm operator's DID is the registered human
   Revocation status    →  confirm Passport not revoked/suspended
   Mandate chain anchor  →  confirm root issuer matches the operator
   Quota / rep score     →  site-specific policy
```

A well-designed site performs **Tier 1 first** (rejects obviously forged traffic) and **Tier 2 second** (applies nuanced policy). The registry must therefore be **disaster-recoverable from cryptographically signed logs alone** — if the live registry is destroyed, Tier 2 must be reconstructable from the historical audit ledger so that already-issued Passports can be re-validated.

---

## 2. ## 🛠️ DETAILED TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION

### 2.1 The Passport Wire Format (What Crosses the Wire)

The Passport is the canonical, signed, JCS-canonicalized JSON object. Every site that supports Web Bot Auth fetches it from a stable URL derived from the agent's DID.

**Passport URL Convention:**
- `did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK` resolves to `https://agents.agentgrid.dev/agent/z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK/passport.json`
- Resolution method: `did:webvh` for the hosted directory, `did:key` for the offline fallback

```jsonc
{
  "@context": [
    "https://www.w3.org/ns/did/v1",
    "https://w3id.org/security/suites/ed25519-2020/v1",
    "https://agentgrid.dev/ns/passport/v1"
  ],
  "id": "did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK",
  "type": "AgentPassport",
  "controller": "did:key:z6MkrJV9braduhYpDgZLE8YB5X7H7ME6fWo3qt3vZ7ZrSa3T",
  "operator": {
    "id": "did:key:z6MkrJV9braduhYpDgZLE8YB5X7H7ME6fWo3qt3vZ7ZrSa3T",
    "displayName": "Jane Operator",
    "verificationMethod": [{
      "id": "did:key:z6MkrJV9braduhYpDgZLE8YB5X7H7ME6fWo3qt3vZ7ZrSa3T#z6MkrJV9braduhYpDgZLE8YB5X7H7ME6fWo3qt3vZ7ZrSa3T",
      "type": "Ed25519VerificationKey2020",
      "publicKeyMultibase": "z6MkrJV9braduhYpDgZLE8YB5X7H7ME6fWo3qt3vZ7ZrSa3T"
    }],
    "reputation": {
      "tier": "verified-human",
      "issuedAt": "2026-05-12T10:00:00Z",
      "issuer": "https://agentgrid.dev/reputation/HUMAN-DID"
    }
  },
  "agent": {
    "displayName": "Research Agent Alpha",
    "created": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
    "publicKey": {
      "id": "did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK#z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK",
      "type": "Ed25519VerificationKey2020",
      "publicKeyMultibase": "z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK"
    },
    "serviceEndpoints": {
      "botAuthKeys": "https://agents.agentgrid.dev/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory/z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK",
      "auditLog": "https://audit.agentgrid.dev/agent/z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK",
      "revocationStatus": "https://agents.agentgrid.dev/revocation/z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK"
    }
  },
  "capabilities": ["browse", "comms", "pay", "deploy", "signup"],
  "status": "active",
  "proof": {
    "type": "Ed25519Signature2020",
    "verificationMethod": "did:key:z6MkrJV9braduhYpDgZLE8YB5X7H7ME6fWo3qt3vZ7ZrSa3T#z6MkrJV9braduhYpDgZLE8YB5X7H7ME6fWo3qt3vZ7ZrSa3T",
    "created": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
    "proofPurpose": "assertionMethod",
    "jws": "eyJhbGciOiJFZERTQSIsImNyaXQiOlsiYjptZW0iXX0..signature_bytes_here"
  }
}
```

### 2.2 The RFC 9421 Signed Request (What the Agent Sends)

Every outbound HTTP request the executor makes carries an RFC 9421 `Signature` and `Signature-Input` header. The signature covers:

- `@method` — POST/GET/etc.
- `@authority` — destination host
- `@path` — request path
- `@query` — canonicalized query parameters
- `content-digest` — SHA-256 of request body
- `x-agentgrid-nonce` — 128-bit random nonce
- `x-agentgrid-timestamp` — RFC 3339 timestamp (±300s window)

**Example outbound request:**

```http
POST /v1/scrape HTTP/2
Host: target.example.com
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Digest: sha-256=:X48E9qOokqqrvdts8nOJRJN3OWDUoyWxBf7kbu9DBPE=:
User-Agent: AgentGrid/1.0 (Agent: Research-Alpha; Operator-DID: did:key:z6Mkr...; Contact: https://agents.agentgrid.dev/contact)
x-agentgrid-nonce: 5fc7d0a4b8e34f9a
x-agentgrid-timestamp: 2026-06-13T12:34:56.789Z
x-agentgrid-mandate-hash: sha-256=:f3b9c2e1...:
Signature-Input: sig1=("@method" "@authority" "@path" "content-digest" "x-agentgrid-nonce" "x-agentgrid-timestamp" "x-agentgrid-mandate-hash");created=1718282096;keyid="did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK#z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK";alg="ed25519";tag="agentgrid-bot-auth"
Signature: sig1=:base64url_ed25519_signature_here:
```

### 2.3 The `.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory` Schema

Each agent publishes its public key directory at a stable, DID-derived URL. The directory is the **substrate of trust** — losing it means sites can no longer verify the agent's signatures.

```jsonc
{
  "@context": "https://agentgrid.dev/ns/bot-auth-directory/v1",
  "id": "did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK",
  "controller": "did:key:z6MkrJV9braduhYpDgZLE8YB5X7H7ME6fWo3qt3vZ7ZrSa3T",
  "verificationMethod": [{
    "id": "#z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK",
    "type": "Ed25519VerificationKey2020",
    "publicKeyMultibase": "z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK",
    "validFrom": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
    "validUntil": "2027-06-12T10:00:00Z"
  }],
  "proof": {
    "type": "Ed25519Signature2020",
    "verificationMethod": "did:key:z6MkrJV9braduhYpDgZLE8YB5X7H7ME6fWo3qt3vZ7ZrSa3T#z6MkrJV9braduhYpDgZLE8YB5X7H7ME6fWo3qt3vZ7ZrSa3T",
    "created": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
    "proofPurpose": "assertionMethod",
    "jws": "eyJhbGciOiJFZERTQSIsImNyaXQiOlsiYjptZW0iXX0..signature_bytes_here"
  }
}
```

**Hosting layout on `agents.agentgrid.dev`:**

```
https://agents.agentgrid.dev/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory
                                                       │
                                                       ├─ /{agent-did-1}/keys.json
                                                       ├─ /{agent-did-2}/keys.json
                                                       └─ /{agent-did-3}/keys.json
```

The agent's DID is the directory namespace — key directories are content-addressed and trivially mirrorable.

### 2.4 The WAF/CDN Integration Pattern (Site-Side)

A site that wants to welcome verified agents implements this check at the edge (Cloudflare Worker, Akamai EdgeWorker, Fastly Compute@Edge):

```typescript
// Cloudflare Worker pseudocode — site-side verification
import { verifySignature, parseSignatureInput } from "@cloudflare/web-bot-auth";

export default {
  async fetch(req: Request, env: Env): Promise<Response> {
    const sigHeader = req.headers.get("Signature");
    const sigInput = req.headers.get("Signature-Input");

    if (!sigHeader || !sigInput) {
      // No signed-agent identity → fall through to standard bot detection
      return handleAnonymously(req, env);
    }

    // 1. Parse keyid from Signature-Input
    const { keyid } = parseSignatureInput(sigInput);
    const agentDid = keyid.split("#")[0];

    // 2. Fetch public key directory (cached 1h at edge)
    const keysResp = await fetch(
      `https://agents.agentgrid.dev/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory/${agentDid}/keys.json`,
      { cf: { cacheTtl: 3600 } }
    );
    if (!keysResp.ok) {
      return challengeWithCaptcha(req, "key_directory_unavailable");
    }
    const keyDir = await keysResp.json();

    // 3. Verify signature cryptographically
    const isValid = await verifySignature(req, keyDir.verificationMethod[0].publicKeyMultibase);
    if (!isValid) {
      return new Response("Invalid signature", { status: 401 });
    }

    // 4. Check revocation status (cached 60s)
    const revokeResp = await fetch(
      `https://agents.agentgrid.dev/revocation/${agentDid}`,
      { cf: { cacheTtl: 60 } }
    );
    const status = await revokeResp.json();
    if (status.revoked) {
      return new Response("Agent revoked", { status: 403 });
    }

    // 5. Verify operator binding (cached 1h)
    const passportResp = await fetch(
      `https://agents.agentgrid.dev/agent/${agentDid}/passport.json`,
      { cf: { cacheTtl: 3600 } }
    );
    const passport = await passportResp.json();
    if (passport.status !== "active") {
      return new Response("Passport not active", { status: 403 });
    }

    // 6. Apply site-specific policy (e.g., per-agent rate limits, rep score)
    const operator = passport.operator;
    if (operator.reputation?.tier === "verified-human") {
      // Tag the request and bypass CAPTCHA
      req = new Request(req, {
        headers: new Headers([
          ...req.headers,
          ["x-verified-agent", agentDid],
          ["x-operator-tier", "verified-human"],
        ]),
      });
      return forwardToOrigin(req, env, { skipChallenge: true });
    }

    return forwardToOrigin(req, env, { requireCaptcha: true });
  },
};
```

### 2.5 The Mandate Hash Header (Proving Authority for the Action)

A site that wants to confirm *what the agent is authorized to do* (not just *who it is*) reads the `x-agentgrid-mandate-hash` header. The hash resolves to a Mandate document on the registry:

```jsonc
{
  "id": "mnd_01HZX8K...",
  "version": 1,
  "issuer": "did:key:z6Mkr...OPERATOR",
  "subject": "did:key:z6Mk...AGENT",
  "capability": "browse",
  "scope": {
    "allowedDomains": ["github.com", "*.example.com"],
    "deniedDomains": [],
    "maxSessionMinutes": 60
  },
  "notBefore": "2026-06-12T00:00:00Z",
  "expires": "2026-12-31T23:59:59Z",
  "proof": { "jws": "..." }
}
```

A site can fetch the Mandate at `https://agents.agentgrid.dev/mandate/{mandate-id}.json` and confirm:
- Issuer DID matches the Passport's operator
- Subject DID matches the signing key
- `target_domain ∈ scope.allowedDomains`
- `expires > now`
- Mandate is not in the revocation list

**But:** disclosing the full Mandate to every site would leak the agent's full authority envelope. The recommended pattern is **selective disclosure via W3C VC Presentation Definitions** — the agent presents a derived Verifiable Credential proving only the relevant claim ("this agent may access `example.com`") without revealing the full Mandate. For v1, sites accept the Mandate hash and fetch the Mandate themselves; selective disclosure is the v2 optimization.

---

## 3. ## ⚠️ FAILURE MODES & TRUST HARDENING

### 3.1 The Registry as a Single Point of Failure

The registry is the lynchpin of the entire Web Bot Auth flow. If `agents.agentgrid.dev` is destroyed, sites lose:
- The Passport documents (operator binding)
- The key directories (signature verification)
- The Mandate documents (authority verification)
- The revocation status (live trust state)

A **catastrophic registry loss** would degrade every already-issued agent to **Tier 1 only** — the agent's own `did:key` is still self-certifying, so signatures verify offline, but sites can no longer confirm operator binding, revocation, or Mandate authority. This is the disaster recovery problem.

### 3.2 Cryptographic Reconstruction From the Audit Ledger

The audit ledger (§8 of the Blueprint) is **append-only, hash-chained, and signed**. It is the *backup of last resort*. Because the Blueprint already requires every agent action to produce a signed ledger entry, the registry can be **fully rebuilt from the ledger**, with these reconstruction steps:

**Step 1 — Replay all `passport.issued` events.**

```sql
-- Pseudo-SQL: extract Passport issuance events from the historical ledger
SELECT block_index, payload, signature
FROM audit_ledger
WHERE event_type = 'passport.issued'
ORDER BY block_index ASC;
```

Each event contains the full Passport document + operator signature. The rebuilder verifies each signature against the agent's `did:key` (extracted from the document) before accepting it.

**Step 2 — Replay all `passport.revoked` events (in order).**

```sql
SELECT block_index, payload->>'did' AS revoked_did, payload->>'revoked_at' AS at
FROM audit_ledger
WHERE event_type = 'passport.revoked'
ORDER BY block_index ASC;
```

Revocations **override** issuance. Apply them in block order. A Passport issued at block 100 and revoked at block 500 has `status='revoked'` in the rebuilt registry.

**Step 3 — Replay all `mandate.issued` events.**

Each event is a self-contained, JCS-canonicalized Mandate with the issuer's signature. Verify signature → re-insert into the `mandates` table.

**Step 4 — Replay all `mandate.revoked` events.**

Same override logic as Passport revocations.

**Step 5 — Re-derive usage tallies from `mandate.usage` events.**

Re-sum spend and counter tallies per `(mandate_id, period_start)`. The rebuilder must replay events in strict block order; the hash chain guarantees order integrity.

**Step 6 — Verify the hash chain end-to-end.**

```typescript
async function verifyChain(ledger: AuditLedger): Promise<boolean> {
  let prevHash = Buffer.alloc(32); // genesis
  for (let i = 0; i < ledger.length; i++) {
    const block = ledger[i];
    if (block.previousHash !== prevHash.toString("hex")) return false;
    const canonical = jcsCanonicalize({
      index: block.index,
      timestamp: block.timestamp,
      eventType: block.eventType,
      payload: block.payload,
      previousHash: block.previousHash,
    });
    const computed = sha256(canonical);
    if (computed.toString("hex") !== block.blockHash) return false;
    if (!verifyEd25519(block.signature, computed, block.agentDid)) return false;
    prevHash = Buffer.from(block.blockHash, "hex");
  }
  return true;
}
```

If the chain verifies, the rebuilt state is **bit-for-bit equivalent** to the original. The Blueprint's §16.2.5 fix (verify at decision time) means every entry's signature was already cryptographically validated; the reconstruction is purely structural.

### 3.3 The Backup Architecture (Three-Tier Defense)

**Tier 1 — Continuous ledger replication (operational backup).**

The audit ledger is **the** ground truth. It is replicated:
- **Write-ahead**: every `policy_decision` and `action` is persisted to a primary Postgres cluster *before* the action is executed (Blueprint §8 failure-mode rule).
- **Streaming replication**: Postgres logical decoding streams every INSERT to:
  - A **hot standby** in a different availability zone (RPO < 5s)
  - A **cold storage bucket** (S3/GCS) in object-lock/compliance mode (immutable, 7-year retention)
  - A **write-once transparency log** (e.g., a daily Merkle root anchored to a public timestamping service — Rekor, Certificate Transparency, or a Bitcoin OP_RETURN)

**Tier 2 — Geographic replication (disaster recovery).**

For the registry's *derived* state (Passport/Mandate/Usage tables), use multi-region Postgres with:
- **Synchronous commit** to a co-region replica
- **Asynchronous streaming** to a third region (e.g., AWS us-east-1 ↔ us-west-2 ↔ eu-west-1)
- **Quarterly DR drill**: spin up the third region from scratch, replay ledger, validate RTO ≤ 1 hour

**Tier 3 — External anchoring (independent of operator).**

The most critical hardening: anchor the audit ledger's Merkle root to a **public, operator-independent medium** so that even the AgentGrid operator cannot backdate or rewrite history.

```typescript
// Daily anchor job — runs at 00:00 UTC
async function anchorDailyMerkleRoot(): Promise<void> {
  const lastBlock = await auditLedger.getLatest();
  const dayStart = startOfDay(lastBlock.timestamp);
  const dayBlocks = await auditLedger.getBlocksSince(dayStart);
  const merkleRoot = computeMerkleRoot(dayBlocks.map(b => b.blockHash));

  // Option A: Sigstore Rekor (public, free, append-only transparency log)
  await fetch("https://rekor.sigstore.dev/api/v1/log", {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      kind: "agentgrid.audit.anchor",
      spec: {
        data: { hash: { algorithm: "sha256", value: merkleRoot } },
        signature: {
          content: base64url(await kms.sign({ did: "did:key:z6Mk...ANCHOR", payload: merkleRoot })),
          public_key: { content: "z6Mk...ANCHOR" }
        }
      }
    })
  });

  // Option B: Bitcoin OP_RETURN via a stamping service
  await stampViaOpenTimestamps(merkleRoot);

  // Option C: Daily digest email to operator (out-of-band, operator-controlled)
  await sendDailyDigest({ merkleRoot, blockRange: [dayBlocks[0].index, dayBlocks.at(-1)!.index] });
}
```

**Operator retention rule**: the operator is required to retain the *original* email with the daily digest; the email is signed by AgentGrid's anchor key, so the operator can cryptographically prove the state as of that day — even if AgentGrid later goes rogue.

### 3.4 Recovery Procedure (RTO / RPO Targets)

| Scenario | Detection | RPO | RTO | Procedure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Registry DB corruption** (single row) | Hash-chain break in audit | 0 (synchronous) | <5 min | Failover to replica; rebuild corrupted row from ledger |
| **Single AZ outage** | Health check failure | 0 (sync replica) | <2 min | DNS failover to co-region replica |
| **Region-wide outage** | Multi-AZ health check failure | <5s (last replicated block) | <30 min | Promote cross-region replica; redirect DNS |
| **Total registry loss** (ransomware, operator error) | Outbound checks fail | <24h (last daily anchor) | <1 hour | **Cold rebuild from audit ledger** + last known Merkle root |
| **Total AgentGrid operator compromise** | Anomalous signing activity | <24h | N/A (trust failure) | Operator publishes operator-key revocation; all sites reject AgentGrid-signed agents; users migrate to self-hosted or alternative operator |

**RPO contract:** The system guarantees that no more than the last 24 hours of state can be lost. This is enforced by daily Merkle anchoring and the immutability of the S3 object-lock bucket.

**RTO contract:** Total cold rebuild from the S3 audit ledger bucket completes in under 1 hour for a registry of 1M Passports / 10M Mandates (single-region, single-machine replay).

### 3.5 The Cold Rebuild Procedure (Disaster Scenario)

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# agentgrid-restore.sh — cold rebuild from immutable audit ledger
set -euo pipefail

LEDGER_BUCKET="${AGENTGRID_LEDGER_BUCKET:-s3://agentgrid-audit-immutable}"
ANCHOR_SERVICE="${AGENTGRID_ANCHOR_SERVICE:-https://rekor.sigstore.dev}"
TARGET_DB="${AGENTGRID_DB_URL:?required}"

echo "[1/7] Downloading audit ledger snapshot"
aws s3 sync "$LEDGER_BUCKET/snapshots/latest/" /tmp/agentgrid-restore/ --no-progress

echo "[2/7] Verifying hash chain end-to-end"
npx agentgrid audit verify --ledger /tmp/agentgrid-restore/ --strict
# Fails on any tamper — refuses to proceed with compromised data

echo "[3/7] Verifying Merkle root against anchor"
DAILY_ROOT=$(npx agentgrid audit merkle-root --since $(date -u -d 'yesterday' +%Y-%m-%d))
curl -fsS "$ANCHOR_SERVICE/api/v1/index/retrieve" --data-binary \
  "{\"hash\":\"$DAILY_ROOT\"}" | jq -e '.uuid' >/dev/null
# Confirms the last anchored state matches the local ledger head

echo "[4/7] Initializing fresh registry database"
psql "$TARGET_DB" -f packages/identity/schema.sql
psql "$TARGET_DB" -f packages/mandate/schema.sql
psql "$TARGET_DB" -f packages/audit/schema.sql
psql "$TARGET_DB" -f packages/policy/schema.sql

echo "[5/7] Replaying passport events"
npx agentgrid restore replay --event passport.issued --target passports
npx agentgrid restore replay --event passport.revoked --target passports
# Inserts new rows for issued, UPDATEs status for revoked — atomic transaction per batch

echo "[6/7] Replaying mandate events and re-deriving usage tallies"
npx agentgrid restore replay --event mandate.issued --target mandates
npx agentgrid restore replay --event mandate.revoked --target mandates
npx agentgrid restore replay --event mandate.usage --target usage_tallies
# Each replay is idempotent — re-runs are safe (skips already-applied block ranges)

echo "[7/7] Verifying rebuilt state against external assertions"
npx agentgrid registry self-check --expect-total-passports $(cat expected_total.txt)
npx agentgrid audit verify --ledger /tmp/agentgrid-restore/ --db "$TARGET_DB" --cross-validate

echo "RESTORE COMPLETE — registry is operational"
```

### 3.6 Identity Spoofing Mitigations (Re-Asserting the 16.2.2 Fix in the Recovery Context)

A cold rebuild from the ledger must re-verify **every** Passport's signature — the rebuild is not a blind copy from the ledger payload, because the agent's private key (which signed the original `passport.issued` event in the ledger) is the same key the agent uses for bot auth. The rebuilder must:

1. Extract the agent's public key from `did:key` (Multibase decode)
2. Re-verify the operator's signature on the Passport document using the operator's public key
3. **Bind the verification key to the Passport's declared `operator`** — the 16.2.2 fix applied during rebuild (rejecting any Passport where `proof.verificationMethod` is spoofed)
4. Verify the root Mandate's `issuerDid === passport.operatorDid` — the 16.2.1 fix

A signature verification step inside the rebuild script is mandatory:

```typescript
async function reimportPassport(event: AuditEvent): Promise<Passport> {
  const passport = event.payload as Passport;

  // 1. Extract public key from DID (self-certifying)
  const agentPubKey = extractEd25519FromDidKey(passport.id);
  if (!agentPubKey) throw new Error("Invalid agent DID format");

  // 2. Extract operator public key from DID
  const operatorPubKey = extractEd25519FromDidKey(passport.operator.id);
  if (!operatorPubKey) throw new Error("Invalid operator DID format");

  // 3. Canonicalize and verify operator's signature
  const canonical = jcsCanonicalize(stripProof(passport));
  const sigValid = verifyEd25519(
    base64UrlDecode(passport.proof.jws.split(".")[2]),
    sha256(canonical),
    operatorPubKey
  );
  if (!sigValid) throw new Error("Operator signature invalid");

  // 4. The 16.2.2 fix — bind verification key to declared operator
  const claimedVerifier = passport.proof.verificationMethod.split("#")[0];
  if (claimedVerifier !== passport.operator.id) {
    throw new Error("16.2.2: proof.verificationMethod must equal operator DID");
  }

  // 5. Apply revocation status from later events (already filtered)
  return passport;
}
```

### 3.7 Replay Attack Mitigations (RFC 9421 + Nonce Tracking)

A signed request is **replayable** unless bound to a one-time-use nonce. The Vault-side request signer must consult a server-side nonce tracker:

```typescript
// Server-side nonce tracker (used by sites to detect replays)
interface NonceTracker {
  record(nonce: string, agentDid: string, ttlSeconds: number): Promise<void>;
  exists(nonce: string): Promise<boolean>;
}

// Implementation — Redis with sliding-window TTL
class RedisNonceTracker implements NonceTracker {
  constructor(private readonly redis: Redis) {}

  async record(nonce: string, agentDid: string, ttlSeconds = 600): Promise<void> {
    const key = `nonce:${nonce}`;
    const exists = await this.redis.set(key, agentDid, "EX", ttlSeconds, "NX");
    if (exists !== "OK") {
      throw new NonceReuseError(`Nonce ${nonce} already used`);
    }
  }

  async exists(nonce: string): Promise<boolean> {
    return (await this.redis.exists(`nonce:${nonce}`)) > 0;
  }
}
```

The agent's HTTP client records each nonce before sending; a duplicate within the TTL window is rejected at the signing layer.

### 3.8 Revocation Propagation (Sub-Second Targets)

Revocation must propagate faster than the rate of new attacks. Architecture:

```
[Operator's Approval App]
   | (revoke action)
   v
[Postgres: UPDATE passports SET status='revoked' WHERE did=$1]
   |
   ├─→ [LISTEN/NOTIFY → WebSocket fanout to all edge nodes]
   ├─→ [Redis pub/sub: agentgrid:revocation:{did} → evicted from all edge caches]
   ├─→ [Cloudflare KV / Workers Cache purge: /revocation/{did}]
   └─→ [WebSub publish to https://agents.agentgrid.dev/revocation/{did}]
```

**Latency budget:**
- Edge cache invalidation: < 500ms globally
- WebSub fanout: < 2s
- Hard TTL on revocation responses: 60s (cache-fail-safe: when the cache misses and the origin is unreachable, the verifier treats the agent as **suspended** until the origin is reachable — fail-closed for high-risk actions, fail-open for read-only)

### 3.9 CAPTCHA Fallback Logic (The Irreducible Minimum)

When a site does not honor Web Bot Auth, the agent falls back to a **single, bounded STEP_UP**. Implementation:

```typescript
async function handleSiteResponse(resp: Response, session: Session): Promise<void> {
  if (resp.status !== 403 && resp.status !== 429) return resp;

  const challenge = parseChallenge(resp);
  if (challenge.type !== "captcha") {
    // Some other 4xx/5xx — surface to operator
    return resp;
  }

  // Single STEP_UP per session per site — not per request
  const dedupKey = `captcha:${session.agentDid}:${session.site}`;
  if (await redis.exists(dedupKey)) {
    // Already pushed once for this site in this session
    // Subsequent challenges go unresolved → 4xx returned to agent
    return new Response("CAPTCHA wall — operator already notified", { status: 503 });
  }
  await redis.setex(dedupKey, 1800, "1"); // 30-min window

  // Push to operator's device
  await pushChannel.send({
    to: session.operatorDid,
    type: "captcha_step_up",
    payload: {
      sessionId: session.id,
      site: session.site,
      challengeUrl: challenge.url,
      actionClass: "unsolvable_bot_wall",
      ttlSeconds: 300,
    },
  });

  // Block the calling tool call until human resolves
  return new Promise((resolve) => {
    session.pendingCaptcha = { resolve, timer: setTimeout(() => {
      resolve(new Response("CAPTCHA timeout", { status: 504 }));
    }, 300_000) };
  });
}
```

The agent **must not** attempt automated CAPTCHA solving — that path is the malicious-bot ecosystem the entire Web Bot Auth protocol exists to replace, and attempting it would (a) be a ToS violation, (b) get the agent's Passport revoked, and (c) destroy the operator's reputation tier.

### 3.10 Threat-Model: Operator Compromise

The most severe failure: AgentGrid's operator key is compromised. Mitigations layered from inside-out:

1. **Operator keys never touch our servers.** The Mandate-signing key lives in the operator's device Secure Enclave (§2.6). Even a full server compromise cannot forge Mandates.
2. **Daily Merkle anchoring to a public log** (Rekor/Certificate Transparency) means the operator cannot backdate the audit ledger — an external observer can prove the state as of any anchored day.
3. **Operator-independent agent verification.** A site that cached a Passport's public key during normal operation can continue to verify the agent's RFC 9421 signatures **even if AgentGrid goes dark** — the agent's `did:key` is self-certifying. Sites that cached the agent's Mandate can continue to enforce authority scope offline.
4. **Reputation portability.** A W3C VC 2.0 reputation credential issued to the operator's DID by an independent reputation authority is verifiable even if AgentGrid's infrastructure is gone. The operator can migrate to an alternative agent-identity provider without re-issuing agent Passports — operator binding is cryptographic, not platform-bound.
5. **Multi-operator competition.** The protocol is open. If AgentGrid as an operator fails, the Passport format is non-proprietary; another operator can host an equivalent registry. The 16.2.1/16.2.2 fixes (operator binding at the Passport level) ensure that any compatible operator's Passports are valid.

### 3.11 Operator Onboarding — The Critical-Step Credential Push

The most fragile human moment: the operator installing the Approval App and registering the device. A single error here means the operator cannot sign Mandates, and the agent is bricked. The flow:

```
1. Operator installs AgentGrid Approval App (iOS/Android/RN desktop)
2. App generates operator keypair in Secure Enclave (non-exportable)
3. App registers operator's DID with the registry:
   POST /v1/operators
   {
     "did": "did:key:z6Mkr...",
     "publicKeyMultibase": "z6Mkr...",
     "deviceAttestation": "<Apple App Attest / Android Key Attestation JWT>"
   }
4. Registry stores the operator + requires email/phone verification
5. Agent signs its first Passport with a one-time registration token
6. Future Mandates are signed by the operator's on-device key
```

**Trust hardening:** the device attestation binds the operator key to a specific hardware enclave. A Mandate signed by a key without a valid device attestation is rejected by the Policy Engine.

---

## 4. Summary: The Verification Protocol End-to-End

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 1. AGENT REGISTRATION                                                  │
│    Agent generates Ed25519 keypair (in Vault)                          │
│    Agent DID = did:key:z6Mk...AGENT                                    │
│    Operator DID = did:key:z6Mkr...HUMAN                                │
│    Passport signed by operator, anchored at registry URL               │
│    Key directory published at /.well-known/http-message-signatures-dir  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                  │
                                  ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 2. EVERY OUTBOUND HTTP REQUEST                                         │
│    Executor builds request (agent never sees the bytes)                │
│    Vault signs RFC 9421 signature with agent's private key             │
│    Nonce + timestamp + content-digest covered                          │
│    Mandate hash header attached (optional)                             │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                  │
                                  ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 3. SITE-SIDE VERIFICATION (Cloudflare/Akamai/Fastly Worker)            │
│    Tier 1 (offline): did:key → Ed25519 pubkey → verify signature      │
│    Tier 2 (online): fetch Passport, Mandate, revocation status        │
│    Apply site policy: tier-based CAPTCHA bypass, rate limits           │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                  │
                                  ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 4. POLICY ENFORCEMENT                                                 │
│    Every consequential action routes through Policy Engine             │
│    ALLOW / ALLOW_WITH_NOTICE / STEP_UP / DENY                          │
│    Every decision → hash-chained audit entry (signed by agent)         │
│    STEP_UP → push to operator's device → signed approval envelope      │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                  │
                                  ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 5. REGISTRY DURABILITY                                                │
│    Audit ledger streamed to immutable S3 + multi-region Postgres       │
│    Daily Merkle root anchored to Rekor + OTS                           │
│    Cold rebuild: 1h RTO from any single source of truth                │
│    Self-checks: hash chain verify, cross-validate against anchors      │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 5. Recovery Runbook: "The Registry Is Gone" Drill

**Scenario:** `agents.agentgrid.dev` is completely destroyed. All Postgres clusters, all S3 buckets, all DNS records. Only the immutable audit ledger (in a third-party custody provider or geo-redundant object-lock bucket) survives.

**Hour 0–1: Containment**
- Operator's on-device Mandate-signing key is **unaffected** (it lives in the Secure Enclave).
- Already-issued Mandates **remain cryptographically valid** — sites that cached the Mandate can still verify them offline.
- Agents **continue to sign RFC 9421 requests** with their `did:key` private keys (held in the Vault / KMS) — sites that cached the public key can still verify the agent's identity offline.
- Degraded mode: sites without cached Passports/Mandates must fail-closed (treat signed-agent traffic as unverified → fall back to standard bot detection).

**Hour 1–2: Cold Rebuild**
- Provision a fresh Postgres cluster in a new region.
- Run `agentgrid-restore.sh` (Section 3.5).
- Re-publish the registry at the same DNS names (with new TLS certs).
- Re-publish all `.well-known` key directories.

**Hour 2–3: Verification**
- Run hash chain verification end-to-end.
- Verify the Merkle root of the rebuilt state matches the last anchored root on Rekor.
- Run a synthetic request from a test agent → confirm site-side verification succeeds.

**Hour 3–24: Propagation**
- Sites that pinned the old registry URLs in their CDN caches will gradually pick up the new origin (TTL ≤ 1h).
- Notify the Cloudflare Bot Auth registry / Akamai / Fastly of the new origin URL so signing-key verifications resume.
- Operator publishes a transparency report: post-mortem, RTO/RPO achieved, evidence of state equivalence.

**Beyond 24h: Continuous Operations**
- Resume daily Merkle anchoring.
- Resume multi-region replication.
- Schedule the next quarterly DR drill.

The protocol is **resilient by construction** because the cryptographic primitives are independent of the hosting infrastructure. The Passport's `did:key` is self-certifying; the Mandate is independently verifiable; the audit ledger is hash-chained; the Merkle anchor is publicly timestamped. Losing the registry is an operational inconvenience, not a cryptographic catastrophe.

---

*End of Registry Backup & Disaster Recovery Specification. This document is the implementation contract for `packages/identity`, `packages/audit`, `packages/policy`, and the `agents.agentgrid.dev` registry infrastructure. Implementation is gated on completion of M0–M2 of the build sequence (Blueprint §12) plus the four production seams A2, A3, E1, D1 detailed in §16.*

```json
{

}
```

```json
  "type": ["VerifiableCredential", "AgentPassport"],

  "validFrom": "2026-06-12T00:00:00Z",

    "type": "Ed25519Signature2020",
    "verificationMethod": "did:key:z6Mk...OPERATOR#key-1",
    "proofPurpose": "assertionMethod",
    "jws": "..."
  }
}
```

**The Mandate VC sent with the request** (for the audit log):
```json
{
  "@context": ["https://www.w3.org/ns/credentials/v2"],
  "type": ["VerifiableCredential", "AgentMandate"],
  "issuer": "did:key:z6Mk...OPERATOR",
  "credentialSubject": {
    "id": "did:key:z6Mk...AGENT",
    "mandateId": "mnd_01H...",
    "capability": "browse",
    "scope": {
      "allowedDomains": ["example.com"]
    }
  },
  "proof": { ... }
}
```

**Kafka/EventBridge topic for SIEM:**
Topic: `agentgrid.audit.signature-events.v1`
Schema (Avro/Protobuf):
```protobuf
message SignatureEvent {
  string event_id = 1;
  string agent_did = 2;
  string operator_did = 3;
  string passport_id = 4;
  string mandate_id = 5; // current active mandate chain root
  int64 timestamp_ms = 6;
  string target_host = 7;
  string path = 8;
  string method = 9;
  bytes request_hash = 10; // sha256 of canonical request
  bytes signature = 11; // ed25519 signature
  bytes previous_audit_hash = 12; // hash chain
  bytes audit_block_hash = 13;
  string decision = 14; // allowed | challenged | denied
  string nonce = 15;
  int64 expires_at_ms = 16;
  string passport_status = 17; // active | suspended | revoked
}
```

**Revocation topic:**
Topic: `agentgrid.revocations.v1`
```protobuf
message RevocationEvent {
  string event_id = 1;
  string subject_did = 2; // passport or mandate being revoked
  string subject_type = 3; // "passport" | "mandate" | "key"
  int64 timestamp_ms = 4;
  string reason = 5;
  bytes signature = 6; // signed by operator
  string operator_did = 7;
}
```

**Site verification endpoint (optional, for richer verification):**
`POST https://agents.yourdomain.com/v1/verify`
Request: `{"signature": "...", "input": "...", "agent_did": "...", "purpose": "..."}`
Response: `{"valid": true, "passport": {...}, "operator_verified": true, "active_mandates": [...], "audit_chain_head": "..."}`

**The handshake flow:**

1. Agent request → carries `Signature-Input` + `Signature` + `Signature-Agent` headers
2. Site sees signed request, decides to honor (per their policy)
3. Site extracts `keyid` from Signature-Input, parses agent DID from Signature-Agent
4. Site fetches `https://agents.yourdomain.com/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory`
5. Site verifies the signature against the public key
6. Site checks its own revocation cache (subscribed to pub-sub feed)
7. Site applies its policy (skip CAPTCHA / challenge / deny)
8. Site (optionally) calls `verify` endpoint for richer info
9. Site's WAF/edge publishes the verification event to its own SIEM
10. AgentGrid's edge receives the same event via mirrored topic
11. Both SIEMs have cryptographically verifiable, hash-chained audit trails

**Pub/Sub architecture for SIEM:**

```
[Agent] --sign--> [Site Edge / WAF] --verify--> [AgentGrid Verify Service]
                       |                                |
                       v                                v
              [Site SIEM Kafka]                 [AgentGrid Kafka Cluster]
                       ^                                ^
                       |                                |
                       +---- [Mirror / Pub-Sub] --------+
                                |
                                v
                       [External SIEMs: Splunk, Datadog, 
                        Chronicle, Sumo, Elastic via Kafka Connect]
```

**The audit bus topics:**
- `agentgrid.signature.events.v1` — every signed request observed
- `agentgrid.revocations.v1` — every revocation
- `agentgrid.mandates.issued.v1` — new mandates
- `agentgrid.mandates.amended.v1` — always-allow expansions
- `agentgrid.audit.blocks.v1` — every hash-chained audit block
- `agentgrid.passports.status.v1` — passport state transitions

**SIEM integration patterns:**
- Kafka Connect with Splunk S2S / HEC sink
- AWS EventBridge → Kinesis → Lambda → Datadog/CloudWatch/SIEM
- Confluent Schema Registry for Avro/Protobuf
- mTLS between AgentGrid and SIEM
- Per-tenant topic naming or partitioning

**Revocation propagation:**
- Operator's app pushes revocation event to AgentGrid control plane
- Control plane writes to audit ledger
- Control plane publishes to `agentgrid.revocations.v1` topic
- All subscribers (sites' edge caches, SIEMs, partner registries) receive within seconds
- Revocation list cache TTL: 60 seconds
- Sites check revocation before allowing action
- Fail-closed for high-risk actions; fail-open for read-only

**CAPTCHA fallback:**
- Site still challenges → agent receives CAPTCHA challenge
- Agent's executor detects "captcha presented" state
- Executor fires `request_approval` with `kind: "captcha_solve"`
- Push notification to human's phone
- Human opens app, sees the CAPTCHA image, solves it
- App returns a one-time signed "captcha_solve_token" bound to the session
- Agent submits the token, continues
- Audit log: "captcha_solved_by_human: {operator_did}"

## 📊 IDENTITY & PROTOCOL DIAGNOSIS

### 1.1 The Core Communication Problem

A random site has no prior trust relationship with AgentGrid, no shared secret, and no way to call back to validate a request in real time without latency penalties. The solution is **asymmetric cryptographic identity at the request level**, anchored to a discoverable public-key directory, with **cryptographic evidence the site can verify offline in O(1)** and **pub-sub fan-out for everything that has to be globally consistent** (revocation, mandate amendment, audit chain heads).

The transport is **RFC 9421 HTTP Message Signatures** (the same wire protocol Cloudflare Web Bot Auth implements, the same primitive IETF `httpbis-http-message-signatures-directory` codifies). The identity object is the **Passport** (W3C DID, Ed25519 public key, operator binding, service endpoints). The signed-authority object is the **Mandate** (also a W3C Verifiable Credential, the same envelope as AP2). Both are signed with **JCS (RFC 8785)** canonicalization to eliminate whitespace/key-order ambiguity.

### 1.2 The Cryptographic Stack (What Proves What)

| What the site needs to know | Cryptographic primitive | Anchor |
|---|---|---|
| "This is the same agent every request" | Ed25519 signature on the request | Public key in `/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory` |
| "This agent is operated by a real human/org" | Operator's Ed25519 signature on the Passport | Operator's key (rotatable, never in agent's reach) |
| "The human actually authorized this capability envelope" | Operator's Ed25519 signature on the Mandate | Operator's key, on-device Secure Enclave |
| "This is not a replay of a previous request" | RFC 9421 `created`/`expires` + 128-bit `nonce` signed as a component | Same signature (in-band) |
| "The agent hasn't been revoked since this request was signed" | Signed-revocation feed on a pub-sub bus the site subscribes to | Operator-signed revocation events |
| "The audit trail hasn't been tampered with" | Hash-chained audit blocks signed by the agent key | `audit_chain_head` field, site can `GET /v1/audit/head` |

The single **Passport keypair from §1** is therefore the same key that signs outbound requests, the same key that signs audit blocks, and the key the site uses to authenticate the agent. **No new identity layer is invented** — the same Ed25519 keypair plays all four roles because the threat models are compatible (any party holding the key can sign for the agent; the agent is the *only* one that holds it, and the operator can revoke it).

### 1.3 The Operator-to-Agent Binding (the "this user" question)

This is the part the GTM playbook's outbound email calls the "vulnerability" — the site must believe the agent is bound to a real human, not just any anonymous keypair. The binding is the **operator signature on the Passport** (`proof` field, §1.3 of the blueprint):

```
verify(operatorSignature, JCS(passport), operatorDID) == true
```

The site retrieves the Passport via the `/.well-known` directory, extracts `passport.issuer == did:key:OPERATOR`, fetches the operator's public key from a verifiable source (the operator's own `did:web` document at `https://operator.example/.well-known/did.json`, or a third-party registry AgentGrid publishes into), and verifies the Passport signature. If it verifies **and** the operator's DID can be traced to a KYC'd entity (via the signed audit ledger's `agent_provisioned` event), the site has non-repudiable proof a human stands behind the agent.

This is exactly the **§16.2.2 vulnerability** — the "Passport Signer Binding Forge" — and the audit pub-sub layer exists to make that forge **detectable in real time**. Every passport that changes status (issued, suspended, revoked, key-rotated) is published to a signed topic; sites that subscribe see a *second* signal that the binding key they're looking at matches the binding key the operator *currently* asserts. Mismatches are alerted and the request is denied.

### 1.4 Why Pub-Sub, Not Polling

Static verification (one-off signature check) is necessary but not sufficient. Three classes of state are **continuously changing** and must reach verifying parties within seconds:

1. **Revocations** (passport killed, key compromised, mandate rescinded).
2. **Mandate amendments** (operator expanded the allowlist via "always-allow merchant" — must be honored on next request, not next poll).
3. **Audit chain heads** (the operator's auditor, the site, and AgentGrid's compliance partner all need the same chain head to detect log tampering).

A **Kafka-compatible event bus** (Confluent Cloud, Redpanda, MSK, or self-hosted) is the only mechanism that delivers these in seconds with cryptographic authenticity, replayable ordering, and fan-out to N sites and M SIEMs without pairwise TLS. Each event is itself signed (Ed25519, JCS-canonicalized payload, chain-linked to the prior event of its kind), so subscribers don't have to trust the broker — they verify the *publisher's* signature on every event.

---

## 🛠️ DETAILED TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION

### 2.1 The Outbound Request — Exact Header Shape (RFC 9421 + Web Bot Auth)

When an AgentGrid agent (browser executor or HTTP client) issues a request to `https://example.com/path?q=1`, the executor's HTTP layer (sitting in front of Playwright/CDP or the `fetch` client) adds three headers, signed via the Vault's `sign()` API:

```
GET /path?q=1 HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
User-Agent: AgentGrid/0.7 (https://agents.agentgrid.dev; +https://agents.agentgrid.dev/operator/did)
Signature-Agent: "did:key:z6MkiTZ2D7YoJgX8r8YK4ZJ1LdCv1v8eX8oNz4oJ3p9b4kB2" "https://agents.agentgrid.dev/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory"
Signature-Input: sig1=("@authority" "signature-agent" "@query-param";keyid="ed25519-2026-06-12T10:00:00Z";alg="ed25519";created=1718300000;expires=1718300060;nonce="a3b2c1d4e5f6";tag="web-bot-auth")
Signature: sig1=:BASE64URL(Ed25519.Sign(privateKey, JCS(signedComponents))):
```

**Field-by-field semantics:**

- **`Signature-Agent`** — a Structured Header (RFC 8941) carrying two sf-strings: (1) the agent's DID, (2) the URL of the directory where the public key can be fetched. Sites parse both; the DID is the identity, the URL is the verification endpoint.
- **`Signature-Input`** — the signed components (the *covered elements* of the request, per §2.5 of RFC 9421), plus metadata: `keyid` (which key in the directory was used — supports rotation), `alg` (Ed25519, currently the only acceptable one in Cloudflare's draft), `created`/`expires` (RFC 3339 timestamps, 60-second validity window), `nonce` (16+ bytes random, 128-bit minimum), `tag` (must be `"web-bot-auth"` for sites to honor the fast lane).
- **`Signature`** — base64url-encoded Ed25519 signature over the JCS serialization of the Signature-Input line.

**Covered components** (mandatory minimum):
- `@authority` — the `Host` header value (or `:authority` pseudo-header for HTTP/2).
- `@query-param` — query string parameters (binds the URL, prevents tampering).
- `signature-agent` — the Signature-Agent header itself (binds the asserted identity into the signature).

**Optional but recommended** (raises trust score, lower CAPTCHA probability):
- `@method`, `@path`, `@scheme` (the request line).
- `signature` (a recursive bind of the Signature header value).
- `user-agent`, `accept`, `accept-language` (anti-tamper for content negotiation).

The executor MUST:
1. Add `created = now()`, `expires = now() + 60s` (configurable, default 60s).
2. Generate `nonce = base64url(randomBytes(16))`.
3. Build the signing string per §2.5 of RFC 9421 (component identifier + whitespace + value + newline, in declared order).
4. Call `vault.sign({ did, payload: jcsBytes })` — the agent never sees the private key.
5. Set the `Signature` and `Signature-Input` headers; `Signature-Agent` was already in the request when signing occurred.

### 2.2 The `.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory` Response

Hosted at the URL declared in `Signature-Agent`. **MUST** be served over HTTPS, **MUST** be JSON, **MUST** carry `Cache-Control: max-age=300, must-revalidate` so revoked keys propagate in ≤5 min even without the pub-sub feed.

```json
{
  "uri": "https://agents.agentgrid.dev/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory",
  "keys": [
    {
      "kty": "OKP",
      "crv": "Ed25519",
      "kid": "ed25519-2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
      "use": "sig",
      "alg": "EdDSA",
      "x": "11qYAYKxCrfVS_7TyWQHOg7hcvPapiMlrwIaaPcHURo",
      "valid_from": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
      "valid_until": "2027-06-12T10:00:00Z",
      "status": "active"
    }
  ],
  "operator": {
    "did": "did:key:z6Mkoperator...",
    "name": "Acme Corp",
    "verification_method": "https://acme.example/.well-known/did.json",
    "kyc_attestation": "https://audits.agentgrid.dev/operators/acme/kyc"
  },
  "agent": {
    "did": "did:key:z6MkiTZ2D7YoJgX8r8YK4ZJ1LdCv1v8eX8oNz4oJ3p9b4kB2",
    "passport_id": "pp_01HXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX",
    "display_name": "Acme Research Agent",
    "audit_endpoint": "https://agents.agentgrid.dev/v1/audit/did:key:z6MkiTZ2D7YoJgX8r8YK4ZJ1LdCv1v8eX8oNz4oJ3p9b4kB2/head",
    "revocation_endpoint": "https://agents.agentgrid.dev/v1/revocations/did:key:z6MkiTZ2D7YoJgX8r8YK4ZJ1LdCv1v8eX8oNz4oJ3p9b4kB2",
    "policy_discovery": "https://agents.agentgrid.dev/.well-known/agent-policy.json"
  }
}
```

Field semantics:
- `keys[].kid` matches the `keyid` in `Signature-Input` — supports **zero-downtime key rotation** (old key valid for `valid_until`, new key already published).
- `keys[].status` — `"active" | "suspended" | "revoked"`. Sites MUST reject `revoked`/`suspended` keys even if `valid_until` is in the future. This is the **second-line revocation defense** (in addition to the pub-sub feed).
- `operator.kyc_attestation` — optional but **strongly recommended for high-trust sites** (banks, financial data). A signed VC asserting the operator passed KYC at a recognized provider (Trulioo/Persona/Onfido). Sites that verify this attestation can apply their highest trust tier.

### 2.3 The Operator's DID Document (the Operator's Own Key)

The operator's key is what makes the Passport (and therefore the agent) traceable to a real person/org. Operator identity is published at the operator's own well-known location:

`https://acme.example/.well-known/did.json`
```json
{
  "id": "did:web:acme.example",
  "verificationMethod": [
    {
      "id": "did:web:acme.example#operator-key-1",
      "type": "Ed25519VerificationKey2020",
      "controller": "did:web:acme.example",
      "publicKeyJwk": {
        "kty": "OKP", "crv": "Ed25519",
        "x": "BASE64URL_PUBLIC_KEY"
      }
    }
  ],
  "authentication": ["did:web:acme.example#operator-key-1"],
  "assertionMethod": ["did:web:acme.example#operator-key-1"]
}
```

Sites verify `passport.proof` using this key. If `passport.issuer != did:web:acme.example` OR the signature doesn't verify against the key in this DID document, the site **denies** — this is the §16.2.2 Passport Signer Binding Forge defense.

### 2.4 The Site-Side Verification Flow (Pseudocode, Drop-In)

```typescript
// executed at the site edge (Cloudflare Worker, Fastly Compute, AWS Lambda@Edge, NGINX subrequest)
async function verifyAgentRequest(req: Request): Promise<{ allow: boolean; reason: string }> {
  const sig = req.headers.get("Signature");
  const sigInput = req.headers.get("Signature-Input");
  const sigAgent = req.headers.get("Signature-Agent");
  if (!sig || !sigInput || !sigAgent) return { allow: false, reason: "no_signature" };

  // 1. Parse Structured Headers
  const input = parseSignatureInput(sigInput);          // RFC 9421 §2.5
  const agent = parseSignatureAgent(sigAgent);          // RFC 8941 sf-list
  if (input.tag !== "web-bot-auth") return { allow: false, reason: "tag_mismatch" };

  // 2. Reject expired / future-dated (60s window; tolerate ±5s clock skew)
  const now = Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000);
  if (input.created > now + 5 || input.expires < now - 5) return { allow: false, reason: "stale_or_future" };

  // 3. Check nonce uniqueness (cache TTL 600s; deny on replay)
  if (await nonceCache.seen(input.nonce)) return { allow: false, reason: "nonce_replay" };
  await nonceCache.mark(input.nonce, ttl: 600);

  // 4. Fetch agent's public-key directory (cached 5 min; checked against pub-sub feed)
  const dir = await fetchJSON(agent.dirUrl);
  const key = dir.keys.find(k => k.kid === input.keyid);
  if (!key || key.status !== "active") return { allow: false, reason: "key_not_active" };

  // 5. Re-canonicalize and verify Ed25519 signature
  const signingString = buildSigningString(req, input.components);
  const ok = await verifyEd25519(key.x, signingString, sig);
  if (!ok) return { allow: false, reason: "bad_signature" };

  // 6. Cross-check against in-memory revocation cache (updated by pub-sub)
  if (revocationCache.isRevoked(agent.did, input.keyid)) {
    return { allow: false, reason: "passport_revoked" };
  }

  // 7. (Optional, high-trust sites) Verify operator KYC attestation
  if (site.requiresKYC && dir.operator.kyc_attestation) {
    const kyc = await fetchJSON(dir.operator.kyc_attestation);
    if (!verifyVC(kyc, dir.operator.verification_method)) {
      return { allow: false, reason: "kyc_failed" };
    }
  }

  // 8. Publish the verification event to the site SIEM (signed)
  await siteSIEM.publish({
    topic: "agentgrid.signature.events.v1",
    key: agent.did,
    value: {
      event_id: ulid(),
      agent_did: agent.did,
      operator_did: dir.operator.did,
      passport_id: dir.agent.passport_id,
      keyid: input.keyid,
      method: req.method,
      host: req.headers.get("host"),
      path: new URL(req.url).pathname,
      nonce: input.nonce,
      created: input.created,
      expires: input.expires,
      decision: "allowed",
      sig_verified: true,
      ts_ms: Date.now()
    }
  });

  return { allow: true, reason: "verified" };
}
```

### 2.5 The Audit Pub-Sub Schema (Kafka / Redpanda / MSK)

**Schema Registry subject:** `agentgrid.signature.events.v1` (Avro or Protobuf; Protobuf shown).

```protobuf
syntax = "proto3";
package agentgrid.audit.v1;

message SignatureEvent {
  string  event_id         = 1;   // ULID
  string  schema_version   = 2;   // "1.0.0"
  string  agent_did        = 3;   // did:key:...
  string  operator_did     = 4;   // did:key:... or did:web:...
  string  passport_id      = 5;   // pp_01H...
  string  active_mandate_id= 6;   // mnd_01H... (root of current chain)
  int64   ts_ms            = 7;
  string  method           = 8;   // GET|POST|...
  string  host             = 9;   // example.com
  string  path             = 10;  // /api/v1/...
  bytes   request_hash     = 11;  // sha256(canonical request)
  bytes   signature        = 12;  // ed25519 sig over (request_hash || ts_ms || event_id)
  string  keyid            = 13;  // matches directory key
  string  nonce            = 14;
  int64   expires_ms       = 15;
  string  decision         = 16;  // "allowed" | "challenged" | "denied"
  string  decline_reason   = 17;  // populated on "denied"
  bytes   prev_chain_hash  = 18;  // hash-chain link (sha256 of prior event)
  bytes   chain_hash       = 19;  // sha256(prev_chain_hash || JCS(payload))
}

message RevocationEvent {
  string  event_id         = 1;
  string  schema_version   = 2;
  string  subject_did      = 3;   // passport or mandate
  string  subject_type     = 4;   // "passport" | "mandate" | "key"
  string  operator_did     = 5;
  int64   ts_ms            = 6;
  string  reason           = 7;   // "operator_initiated" | "compromise_suspected" | "key_rotation"
  string  superseded_by    = 8;   // for key rotation: new kid
  bytes   signature        = 9;   // operator-signed
  bytes   prev_chain_hash  = 10;
  bytes   chain_hash       = 11;
}

message MandateAmendmentEvent {
  string  event_id         = 1;
  string  schema_version   = 2;
  string  mandate_id       = 3;
  string  operator_did     = 4;
  int64   ts_ms            = 5;
  string  amendment_type   = 6;   // "add_merchant" | "raise_cap" | "extend_ttl" | "narrow_scope"
  string  field            = 7;   // JSON pointer
  string  new_value        = 8;   // JCS
  bytes   signature        = 9;   // operator-signed
}

message AuditBlockEvent {
  string  event_id         = 1;
  string  schema_version   = 2;
  string  agent_did        = 3;
  int64   block_index      = 4;
  bytes   previous_hash    = 5;
  bytes   block_hash       = 6;
  int64   ts_ms            = 7;
  bytes   signature        = 8;   // agent-signed
  string  event_type       = 9;   // "policy_decision" | "action" | "approval" | "cred_use"
}
```

**Topic layout (production):**

| Topic | Partitions | Retention | Consumers | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `agentgrid.signature.events.v1` | 64 (hash by `agent_did`) | 30 days | Sites' edges, AgentGrid compliance, partner SIEMs | Every observed request |
| `agentgrid.revocations.v1` | 16 (hash by `operator_did`) | Permanent (Compacted: keep last + delete tombstones) | All site edges, SIEMs | Revocation fanout |
| `agentgrid.mandates.amendments.v1` | 16 | Permanent | Sites' policy caches, AgentGrid policy engine | "Always-allow" expansions |
| `agentgrid.audit.blocks.v1` | 32 (hash by `agent_did`) | Permanent | Operator dashboard, external auditors | Hash-chained ledger |
| `agentgrid.passports.status.v1` | 8 | Permanent | All site edges | Passport state transitions |
| `agentgrid.audit.anchors.v1` | 4 | Permanent | External transparency logs (Sigstore Rekor, certificate transparency) | Daily Merkle root |

**Producer settings (AgentGrid side):**
- `acks=all`, `min.insync.replicas=3`, `enable.idempotence=true`.
- `compression.type=zstd`, `linger.ms=10`.
- **mTLS** to the broker; per-tenant service principal; Kafka ACLs restrict each tenant to their own prefix.
- **Schema Registry** (Confluent) for forward/backward compatibility (`compatibility=BACKWARD`).

**Consumer settings (site / SIEM side):**
- `isolation.level=read_committed` (no unverified events).
- `auto.offset.reset=latest` for revocations (only care about new ones); `earliest` for audit blocks.
- 30s max poll interval; 3 retries with exponential backoff (1s, 3s, 9s); dead-letter topic `*.dlq.v1` for poison messages.

### 2.6 The Cloudflare WAF / Bot Management Integration

Cloudflare already implements the verifying side. The site configures a **WAF custom rule**:

```
(http.request.headers["signature-input"][0] contains "tag=\"web-bot-auth\"" and
 http.request.headers["signature-agent"][0] contains "did:key:")
```

This rule is matched by Cloudflare's `cf.bot_management.score` being replaced / augmented by a "verified agent" score. The recommended pattern (per Cloudflare's `web-bot-auth` library):

1. **Bot Management → Verified Bots** → add `agentgrid.dev` to the allowlist (this is the operator-side allowlist; sites can opt in globally or per-tenant).
2. **Transform Rules** → if a request has a valid `Signature` and `Signature-Agent`, set `cf.client_bot_category = "verified_agent"`.
3. **Custom rule** → if `cf.client_bot_category == "verified_agent"`, skip the Managed Challenge and any Super Bot Fight Mode rules (or apply the site's chosen "verified-agent" policy).
4. **Logpush** → push `AgentgridSignatureVerified` events to the site's SIEM via Cloudflare Logpush to Kafka (or R2/S3 → Kafka Connect).

The site's policy (per the §3.3 decision table) is then:
- **Verified + active mandate + within scope** → skip CAPTCHA, allow.
- **Verified + suspended passport** → deny.
- **Verified + new merchant / over threshold** → step-up page (hosted Turnstile with the agent's known-good signature as the "I am not a robot" token; falls back to the human's approval flow described in §7).
- **Unverified (no signature)** → current behavior (CAPTCHA / block).

### 2.7 The Pub-Sub to External SIEM Bridge

The AgentGrid broker is a **producer** to the bus; **SIEMs and sites are consumers**. A reference bridge to the three most common enterprise SIEMs:

**→ Splunk (via Splunk Connect for Kafka or HEC):**
```
splunk-connect-kafka:
  topics: agentgrid.signature.events.v1, agentgrid.revocations.v1, agentgrid.audit.blocks.v1
  tasks.max: 4
  filters:
    - topic: agentgrid.signature.events.v1
      type: avro
      splunk.hec.uri: https://splunk.example.com:8088
      splunk.hec.token: ${SPLUNK_HEC_TOKEN}
      splunk.index: agentgrid
      splunk.sourcetype: agentgrid:signature
```

**→ Datadog (via Datadog Agent + Kafka consumer or direct Logs API):**
- Run `datadog-agent` with the `kafka_consumer` integration, configured with the bootstrap servers and the topic list.
- Or forward via `logs-config` to a Lambda that calls `POST https://http-intake.logs.datadoghq.com/v1/input` with `ddtags=agentgrid,env:prod`.

**→ Google Chronicle (via Pub/Sub bridge):**
- Deploy a Kafka Connect sink that publishes to `projects/{tenant}/topics/agentgrid-import`.
- Chronicle's native Kafka connector then pulls.

**→ Generic OpenTelemetry (vendor-neutral):**
- Run the `opentelemetry-collector-contrib` with the `kafka` receiver and the `forward` exporter; emit as OTLP logs to any OTLP endpoint (Datadog, New Relic, Elastic, Sumo, Loki, Honeycomb).

**Authenticity across the bridge:** the consumer-side processor MUST re-verify the producer's signature on every event (`producer.public_key` is published in the directory at `https://agents.agentgrid.dev/.well-known/audit-publisher.json`, rotated via the same key-directory versioning as agent keys). This protects against a compromised bridge or man-in-the-middle.

### 2.8 DNS / TXT Schema for Operator Onboarding

Operators run a one-time DNS challenge proving control of their domain:

```
_acme-agent._agentgrid.acme.example. 300 IN TXT "v=agentgrid1; p=https://acme.example/agentgrid/proof; k=ed25519:11qYAYKxCrfVS_7TyWQHOg7hcvPapiMlrwIaaPcHURo"
```

The `p=` URL serves a signed token (Ed25519, JCS) binding the operator's domain to a specific agent public key. This is checked at provisioning and at every key rotation; it is the **proof-of-control anchor** for `did:web` resolution.

### 2.9 The On-Demand Verification Endpoint (for High-Risk Sites)

Some sites cannot or do not want to maintain a local revocation cache. They call a single endpoint at request time:

`POST https://agents.agentgrid.dev/v1/verify`
```json
{
  "agent_did": "did:key:z6MkiTZ2...",
  "request_hash": "sha256-of-canonical-request",
  "nonce": "a3b2c1d4...",
  "created": 1718300000,
  "expires": 1718300060
}
```

**Response:**
```json
{
  "valid": true,
  "passport_status": "active",
  "operator_verified": true,
  "operator_kyc_level": "enhanced",
  "active_mandate_chain": ["mnd_01H...root", "mnd_01H...child"],
  "mandate_scope_for_host": {
    "allowed": true,
    "step_up_required": false
  },
  "audit_chain_head": "7c4a8d09ca3762af61e59520a...",
  "ts_ms": 1718300012345,
  "server_signature": "BASE64URL_ED25519_SIG"
}
```

The response is **signed by AgentGrid's TLS-terminating edge** (mutual-TLS cert pinned by the site), latency budget 200ms p99. Sites SHOULD cache the response keyed by `(agent_did, request_hash, nonce)` for the nonce's TTL to amortize load.

---

## ⚠️ FAILURE MODES & TRUST HARDENING

### 3.1 Replay Attack Mitigation (RFC 9421 Section 7.2 + Nonce Cache)

**Threat:** an attacker intercepts a signed request (e.g., from a leaky proxy) and replays it verbatim.

**Mitigations layered:**

1. **`expires` window (60s default).** A replayed request outside the window is rejected by `input.expires < now`. Sites SHOULD reduce to 30s for high-value endpoints, increase to 120s for low-risk reads.
2. **128-bit nonce + server-side cache.** The site maintains a nonce cache (Redis, with 10-min TTL, ~1KB per 10K nonces). Each nonce is stored with a tombstone; the first sighting is allowed, all subsequent are denied. The cache is sharded by `agent_did` for horizontal scale.
3. **Mandatory `signature-agent` component binding.** The signed components include `signature-agent`, so a replay that swaps the agent DID is signature-invalid.
4. **Request hash recorded in audit log.** The audit ledger records the SHA-256 of the canonical request, so a replay — even if accepted by one site — is detectable by AgentGrid's own SIEM (two events with the same request_hash within the nonce window trigger a `replay_detected` event).
5. **HTTP/2 + HTTP/3 stream binding.** Optional: bind the signature to the connection's TLS exporter (RFC 9266), making replay across connections impossible. Disabled by default; opt-in for high-stakes APIs.

**Detection signal (pub-sub):** `agentgrid.audit.anomalies.v1` topic emits `{"type":"replay_detected", "agent_did":"...", "nonce":"...", "first_seen_ts":..., "second_seen_ts":...}` when the same nonce is observed twice. Sites that subscribe can immediately revoke trust in the agent.

### 3.2 Identity Spoofing & the §16.2.2 Passport Signer Binding Forge

**Threat:** attacker forges a Passport by tampering with `proof.verificationMethod` to point to a key they control.

**Mitigations layered:**

1. **Server-side re-derivation of binding key (already in `verifyPassport` after the patch):** the verification key is *not* taken from `proof.verificationMethod`; it is looked up at the operator's DID document endpoint and asserted equal to `passport.issuer`. Sites that follow the §2.4 verification flow do the same.
2. **Passport + operator DID document cross-check (defense in depth):** the agent's `.well-known` directory response (§2.2) is itself signed by AgentGrid's publisher key, and embeds the operator's DID. The site fetches the operator's DID document, extracts the public key, re-derives the expected Passport ID, and compares against the Passport referenced in the directory. Any mismatch → deny + alert.
3. **Pub-sub tamper-evident passport registry:** every passport state change (`issued`, `key_rotated`, `suspended`, `revoked`) is published to `agentgrid.passports.status.v1` with an operator signature. Sites that subscribe have a *realtime* view of the canonical passport. A spoofed passport that disagrees with the bus is detected within seconds.
4. **Hash-chained passport log:** the passport registry itself is a hash-chained, signed append-only log (the same `audit_blocks.v1` topic). A site can fetch the chain head at `audit_endpoint` and verify that the passport the agent just presented is included in the chain at the expected index. If it's not, the Passport is fabricated.
5. **COSE-signed Passport (optional, for high-assurance sites):** instead of a JSON+JCS Passport, emit a COSE_Sign1 (RFC 8152) compact representation. COSE is a binary, tag-protected format that is harder to tamper with in transit and parses deterministically.

### 3.3 Key Compromise & Rotation

**Threat:** an agent's private key is exfiltrated (Vault breach, side-channel, mis-issued KMS key).

**Mitigations layered:**

1. **Sign-only Vault API (architectural, §4.1):** the agent never holds raw key bytes. A compromise requires a Vault breach, not an agent prompt-injection.
2. **HSM / KMS-backed production key (Seam A3, §16.4):** Ed25519 keys in AWS KMS / GCP KMS / HSM. The signing key is generated inside the HSM, never exported, used only via `Sign()` API calls.
3. **Short token validity (60s default, §6):** even a compromised key has a 60-second usable window per signed request.
4. **Pre-publish rotation:** the directory carries both `kid` and `valid_until`. When the operator rotates, the new `kid` is published *before* the old one expires. Both work for a 24-hour overlap window. The transition is published as a `MandateAmendmentEvent` on the bus with `amendment_type="key_rotated"`.
5. **Compromise protocol (one-shot):** on suspected compromise, the operator's app issues a `RevocationEvent` with `reason="compromise_suspected"`. Bus propagates in <2s to all subscribers. The Passport is tombstoned; new Mandates are issued against the new key. A `post_quantum_migration` event (optional, when ML-DSA / Dilithium is shipped) signals the verification algorithm change.
6. **Forensic chain:** the audit ledger records every signature event with a `request_hash`; post-compromise, the operator's SIEM replays the chain to identify *which* signed requests were forged (any request_hash that doesn't trace to a real outbound HTTP call by the agent process).

### 3.4 Mandate Chain Verification (the "16.2.2" Pattern Generalized)

The same forging pattern that hit the Passport can hit the Mandate chain (a child mandate that violates attenuation, or a chain that doesn't root at the operator). The audit bus is the **second line of defense**:

- Every Mandate issuance, amendment, and revocation is published to `agentgrid.mandates.amendments.v1`.
- Subscribers maintain an in-memory view of the active mandate graph (rooted at `passport.issuer`).
- When a request arrives, the site (or AgentGrid's policy engine) re-validates the chain against the bus view; a chain that disagrees with the bus is rejected as forged.
- A scheduled **chain auditor** (AgentGrid internal) continuously re-validates every active Mandate chain against the bus, alerting on inconsistencies within 30s.

### 3.5 Revocation Propagation (Latency Budgets)

| Class of revocation | Bus latency target | End-to-end (operator tap → site blocks) | Failure-mode if late |
|---|---|---|---|
| Passport suspended | <1s p99 | <3s p99 | Forged agent impersonates legitimate user for ≤3s window |
| Passport revoked (compromise) | <1s p99 | <3s p99 | Same as above; the chain forensics mitigates impact |
| Mandate amended (cap lowered) | <5s p99 | <15s p99 | Agent spends within the old cap for up to 15s |
| Key rotated | <5s p99 | <15s p99 | Old key valid for the overlap window; bus confirms transition |
| Operator-account locked (passphrase forgotten / device stolen) | <1s p99 | <3s p99 | Worst case; lock-and-roll is the safe default |

**Site-side subscription resilience:**
- The site edge **MUST** maintain a local in-memory revocation cache updated by the bus.
- The cache MUST be **fail-closed** for high-risk actions (deny if cache is stale >60s and bus is unreachable).
- The cache MAY be **fail-open** for read-only actions (allow if cache is stale and bus is unreachable, but log loudly and revalidate on next bus reconnect).
- The directory endpoint (`/.well-known`) is the **last-line fallback**: even with the bus down, the directory's `keys[].status` field carries the revocation, and sites that fetch it on every request (with 5-min cache) are protected within 5 minutes.

**Backpressure & partition tolerance:**
- The bus is a CRDT-style compacted topic: tombstones are kept for 7 days, then compacted. A site that reconnects after a 7-day outage must re-fetch the directory (forced by the cache TTL).
- Idempotent consumers: the `event_id` (ULID) is the dedup key. Sites that process the same revocation twice are safe.

### 3.6 Rate Limiting (Per-Operator, Per-Agent, Per-Mandate, Per-Target)

Rate limits are **enforced at the bus-consumer side** (the site), with a **signed counter** published back to `agentgrid.rate.counters.v1` so the agent's Policy Engine can also see approaching limits:

```
per_agent:   100 req/s   (HTTP 429 over)
per_operator: 1000 req/s across all agents of an operator
per_mandate:  defined in mandate.scope.rateLimit (default 10/s for write actions)
per_target:   negotiated with the site (e.g., 5 req/s to /api/expensive)
```

When a limit is hit, the site's response carries a `Retry-After` header AND a signed rate-limit receipt that the agent's audit log records (so the operator can later adjust mandates to avoid the limit).

### 3.7 CAPTCHA / Turnstile Fallback (the §6 Graceful Degradation)

When a site doesn't honor signed agents (or the verification fails for any reason), the agent receives a **challenge**. The fallback is **not** automated CAPTCHA solving — that is detection-evasion and is explicitly forbidden by the design.

**The fallback protocol:**

1. The site's response includes a `Link: <https://challenges.example.com/captcha?token=...>; rel="captcha-challenge"` header.
2. The agent's executor detects the challenge, classifies it (Turnstile, hCaptcha, reCAPTCHA, image-based, audio-based), and checks if it's solvable by an out-of-band human-approval flow.
3. If yes, the executor calls the **on-device approval app** with `kind: "captcha_solve"`, including the challenge URL, a screenshot (if image-based), and a `session_token` that binds the human's solution to the specific request.
4. The human opens the app, sees the challenge, solves it (types the text, selects the images), and signs the solution with their on-device operator key.
5. The app returns to the executor a **signed captcha-solve receipt**:
   ```json
   {
     "session_token": "...",
     "challenge_url": "https://challenges.example.com/captcha?token=...",
     "solution": "<user-typed>",
     "operator_did": "did:key:...",
     "ts_ms": 1718300012345,
     "signature": "BASE64URL_ED25519_SIG"
   }
   ```
6. The executor submits the solution to the challenge endpoint, with the original signed request headers intact (re-uses them; the solution is a new signed component).
7. The audit log records `event_type="captcha_solved_by_human"` with the receipt, the operator_did, and the session_token. The event is published to `agentgrid.audit.blocks.v1` — proof that a human touched this flow.

**Failure modes:**
- **Human unreachable** (offline, asleep) → action fails closed (deny). The agent parks the task in the approval queue.
- **CAPTCHA is unclassifiable / requires audio call / live agent** → STEP_UP to the human with explicit "I need you to take this action" notification.
- **Repeated CAPTCHA walls** → the bus emits `agentgrid.audit.anomalies.v1` with `type="captcha_flooding_target"`; the agent's policy engine can throttle or pause the agent until the human reviews the target allowlist.
- **CAPTCHA solve rate > N/day per target** → the operator's dashboard surfaces "your agent is hitting captchas on Site X; consider (a) the site's API, (b) adding the site to the no-browse list, (c) contacting the site about Web Bot Auth."

### 3.8 Bus Integrity & Compromise Recovery

**Threat:** AgentGrid's own bus signing key is compromised; an attacker publishes a forged revocation.

**Mitigations:**
- The bus publisher key is in HSM; the same threat surface as the agent keys (i.e., the Vault is the only place a key lives).
- The bus is **append-only with a Merkle root** published to external transparency logs (Sigstore Rekor, certificate transparency). A consumer that re-validates the Merkle root against the transparency log detects retroactive tampering.
- Sites that don't trust AgentGrid's bus can run **independent verification**: re-derive the audit chain head from the directory and the agent's public key, cross-check against the bus state, alert on divergence.
- **Dual-publisher redundancy:** every event is co-signed by the AgentGrid publisher key AND a quorum of independent witnesses (e.g., 2-of-3 of a multi-region publisher). Consumers ignore events that don't have the witness signatures.

### 3.9 The "The 16.2.2 Vulnerability" in the Pub-Sub Context

The §16.2.2 vulnerability (Passport Signer Binding Forge) is **observable on the bus** as an anomaly: a Passport is published to `agentgrid.passports.status.v1` with `event_type="key_rotated"`, but the bus consumer's view of the operator's DID document still shows the *old* key. The bus emits an `anomaly: "passport_key_divergence"` event within 30s. The site's local cache treats the new key as suspect, the agent is throttled, and the operator is notified.

A real-time bus thus turns a **silent forgery** into a **loud divergence** — the attacker's forged passport may pass one isolated verification, but the bus-mediated multi-party view makes it detectable in seconds. This is the pub-sub layer's principal contribution: not just log shipping to SIEMs, but **distributed tamper detection** as a side effect of the same events.

---

### Quick-Reference Implementation Checklist

```
[ ] 1. Sign outbound requests with RFC 9421 + web-bot-auth tag (60s expiry, 128-bit nonce)
[ ] 2. Host /.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory; cache 5 min, mTLS to sites
[ ] 3. Operator's did:web DID document at /.well-known/did.json; Ed25519; rotation-aware
[ ] 4. Provision Kafka topics (signature.events, revocations, mandate.amendments, audit.blocks, passports.status)
[ ] 5. Producer: idempotent, JCS-canonical, Ed25519-signed, mTLS, Schema Registry
[ ] 6. Consumer sites: nonce cache (Redis, 10 min TTL), revocation cache (in-mem, fail-closed for writes)
[ ] 7. Cloudflare WAF: signature-input presence → verified_bot → skip challenges
[ ] 8. Splunk / Datadog / Chronicle / OTel bridge: re-verify producer sig on every event
[ ] 9. On-device captcha-solve flow: signed receipt, audit logged, anomaly on flood
[ ] 10. Daily Merkle root → Sigstore Rekor / certificate transparency
[ ] 11. §16.2.2 detection: bus-driven key divergence check on every passport state change
[ ] 12. Rate limits: per-agent, per-operator, per-mandate, per-target; signed counters back to agent
[ ] 13. Chaos drill: kill broker, verify sites fail-closed on writes; kill Vault, verify sign-only API denies key export
[ ] 14. Daily attestation: 2-of-3 publisher witness signatures on every event; consumers drop anomalies
```

---
