# AgentGrid Verified Agent Negotiation Protocol (AVANP) v1.0
## Standards Proposal: `robots.txt` Extensions + RFC 9421 Profile for Accountable Agent Identification

---

## 📊 IDENTITY & PROTOCOL DIAGNOSIS

### The Negotiation Substrate

The "anti-scraping negotiation" is fundamentally a **bidirectional trust handshake** between three principals that have never met:

1. **The remote site** (Resource Server, RS) — owner of the URL.
2. **The AgentGrid agent** (Client, C) — operating under a delegated human authority.
3. **The AgentGrid Registry** (Identity Provider, IdP) — discoverable via DNS, anchored to the agent's DID.

The negotiation succeeds when the site is convinced, with cryptographic certainty, that the request originates from **(a) a non-revoked agent, (b) operating under a human-issued Mandate, (c) scoped only to actions that Mandate explicitly permits**. The site must be able to verify all three properties in **sub-50ms p99** at the WAF/CDN edge — without consulting the human, without holding a shared secret with the agent, and without trusting any opaque trust-mark issuer beyond a published, cacheable key directory.

### Cryptographic Primitives

| Primitive | Standard | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Asymmetric identity | W3C DID `did:key` (v1), `did:web` (v2) | Self-certifying agent identifier; the identifier *is* the public key |
| Request signing | **RFC 9421** HTTP Message Signatures | Cryptographic binding of every HTTP request to the agent's private key |
| Canonicalization | **RFC 8785** JCS | Deterministic JSON byte representation; eliminates signature mismatch from whitespace/key ordering |
| Signature algorithm | **Ed25519** via `@noble/ed25519` | 64-byte signatures, 32-byte keys, fast verification, side-channel safe |
| Mandate representation | W3C VC 2.0 with custom `AgentCapability` type | Portable, verifiable, cryptographically-tied-to-issuer assertion of authority |
| Key directory | RFC 8615 `.well-known` + IETF `http-message-signatures-directory` draft | HTTPS-served, cacheable, deterministic JSON discovery document |
| Transport security | TLS 1.3 (mandatory) | Underlying confidentiality + server authentication |
| Revocation | Cached `StatusList2021` (W3C VC) with 60s TTL | Near-real-time revocation at edge |

### The Handshake Lifecycle

```
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  PHASE 0 — POLICY DISCOVERY (cold start, ~once per site per 24h)   │
   │  Agent → GET https://site/.well-known/agent-policy.json            │
   │  Agent ← JSON manifest declaring: trust protocol version,           │
   │          acceptable operator DIDs, required capabilities,          │
   │          rate limits, challenge fallback policy                    │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  PHASE 1 — KEY DISCOVERY (cold start, ~once per agent per 24h)     │
   │  Agent → GET https://agentgrid.io/.well-known/                    │
   │             http-message-signatures-directory                     │
   │            ?agent=did:key:z6Mk...AGENT                            │
   │  Agent ← {keys:[{kid, kty, crv, x, alg}], status, mandate_refs}   │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  PHASE 2 — SIGNED REQUEST (every request, ~5ms overhead)           │
   │  Agent → GET /resource HTTP/1.1                                  │
   │         Signature-Input: sig1=("@authority" "@path" "agent-       │
   │           identity" "agent-mandate" "agent-nonce"                  │
   │           "agent-timestamp");created=...;expires=...;             │
   │           keyid="did:key:z6Mk...AGENT#key-1";tag="avnp-v1"        │
   │         Signature: sig1=:BASE64(Ed25519.sign(JCS(signed))):       │
   │         Agent-Identity: did:key:z6Mk...AGENT                      │
   │         Agent-Operator: did:key:z6Mk...HUMAN                      │
   │         Agent-Mandate: mnd_01HXYZ;cap="browse:read"               │
   │         Agent-Nonce: a3f1-4b2e-...                                │
   │         Agent-Timestamp: 2026-06-13T12:00:00Z                     │
   │         User-Agent: AgentGrid/1.0 (compatible; +https://...)      │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  PHASE 3 — EDGE VERIFICATION (WAF/Worker, sub-50ms)               │
   │  1. Parse `Signature-Input` and `Signature` headers                │
   │  2. Reconstruct signed components from request                     │
   │  3. JCS-canonicalize and verify Ed25519 signature                 │
   │  4. Resolve agent DID → fetch + cache key directory (1h TTL)      │
   │  5. Check agent StatusList2021 (60s TTL) → must be "active"      │
   │  6. Fetch Mandate VC (5min TTL) → verify operator signature       │
   │  7. Confirm root Mandate.issuer == Mandate.operator (16.2.2 fix)  │
   │  8. Confirm Mandate.subject == Agent-Identity header (16.2.2 fix) │
   │  9. Confirm requested capability ⊆ Mandate.scope                   │
   │  10. Check rate-limit budget for (operator, capability, site)      │
   │  11. If all pass → forward, log "agent_verified" decision          │
   │      If any fail → fall back to site's standard bot policy        │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### The Three-Way Binding (Anti-16.2.2)

The 16.2.2 vulnerability — **passport signer binding forge** — occurs when verification accepts a passport whose `proof.verificationMethod` is self-asserted rather than anchored to the operator's identifier. The negotiation protocol defeats this by enforcing a **tripartite binding invariant at every request**:

```
BINDING INVARIANT (evaluated at edge on every signed request):

  Let:
    A = Agent-Identity header  (e.g., "did:key:z6Mk...AGENT")
    O = Agent-Operator header  (e.g., "did:key:z6Mk...HUMAN")
    M = Agent-Mandate header   (e.g., "mnd_01HXYZ")
    SigK = public key recovered from `Signature` header

  REQUIRE:
    1. verify(SigK, JCS(signed_components))   == true          [request authenticity]
    2. resolve(A) == {keys: [{kid: ..., pub: SigK}], ...}     [key-identifier binding]
    3. resolve(A).operator == O                               [agent-operator binding]
    4. resolve(O).status == "active"                          [operator not revoked]
    5. resolve(M).issuer == O  AND  resolve(M).subject == A   [mandate root-binding]
    6. resolve(M).status == "active"  AND  now ∈ [nbf, exp]  [mandate validity]
    7. requested_capability ⊆ resolve(M).scope.capabilities   [scope intersection]
    8. resolve(M).step_up_threshold  (STEP_UP is enforced server-side, not requested)

  Any single failure → DENY with HTTP 403 + structured error
```

This is the cryptographic cure for 16.2.2: the `Signature` proves request authenticity, the DID resolution proves key-identifier binding, the Mandate resolution proves the agent cannot self-issue authority, and the chain `M.issuer == O == A.operator` proves the operator is human-anchored.

---

## 🛠️ DETAILED TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION

### 1. The `robots.txt` Extension Grammar (ABNF)

The proposal extends the existing [Google Robots.txt Specification](https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots-text-spec) (May 2022, RFC 9309 compatible) with a new top-level directive family. Extensions are namespaced with the `Avnp-` prefix to avoid collision with vendor-specific directives already in the wild.

```abnf
robots_txt            = *(line / group)
group                 = (user_agent / allow / disallow / sitemap / crawl_delay
                       / avnp_directive) *(allow / disallow / crawl_delay
                       / avnp_directive)
user_agent            = "User-agent" ":" *(WS token) EOL
allow                 = "Allow" ":" *(WS path) EOL
disallow              = "Disallow" ":" *(WS path) EOL
crawl_delay           = "Crawl-delay" ":" *(WS number) EOL
sitemap               = "Sitemap" ":" *(WS URI) EOL

avnp_directive        = avnp_protocol / avnp_discovery / avnp_accept_ops
                      / avnp_require_cap / avnp_forbid_cap / avnp_rate
                      / avnp_audit_min / avnp_challenge

avnp_protocol         = "Avnp-Protocol" ":" *(WS token) EOL
                        ; e.g., "Avnp-Protocol: agentgrid/1.0"
avnp_discovery        = "Avnp-Discovery" ":" *(WS URI) EOL
                        ; absolute HTTPS URL of the .well-known/agent-policy.json
avnp_accept_ops       = "Avnp-Accept-Operators" ":" operator_list EOL
operator_list         = ("any-verified" / (DID *(WS "," WS DID)))
avnp_require_cap      = "Avnp-Require-Capability" ":" *(WS capability) EOL
avnp_forbid_cap       = "Avnp-Forbid-Capability" ":" *(WS capability) EOL
avnp_rate             = "Avnp-Rate-Limit" ":" capability_rate *(WS "," WS capability_rate)
capability_rate       = (capability / "*") ":" number "/" time_window
avnp_audit_min        = "Avnp-Audit-Minimum" ":" ("none" / "signed" / "hash-chained" / "external-anchored") EOL
avnp_challenge        = "Avnp-Challenge-Fallback" ":" ("captcha" / "deny" / "step-up") EOL
```

### 2. Concrete `robots.txt` Example

```robots.txt
# Standard crawling rules
User-agent: *
Disallow: /private/
Disallow: /api/internal/
Crawl-delay: 10

# ============ AgentGrid Verified Agent Negotiation ============
Avnp-Protocol: agentgrid/1.0
Avnp-Discovery: https://example.com/.well-known/agent-policy.json

# We accept any agent whose operator is KYC'd + registered in the
# AgentGrid registry. To lock to specific operators, list their DIDs:
Avnp-Accept-Operators: any-verified
# Avnp-Accept-Operators: did:key:z6Mki...OPERATOR_A,did:key:z6Mkj...OPERATOR_B

# Required: agent must hold a Mandate with these capabilities
Avnp-Require-Capability: browse:read
Avnp-Require-Capability: pay:create

# Forbidden: even with a Mandate, this is hard-blocked
Avnp-Forbid-Capability: *:irreversible
Avnp-Forbid-Capability: *:delete

# Rate limits (per operator × capability × site)
Avnp-Rate-Limit: *:60/minute, browse:read:600/minute, pay:create:10/minute

# Minimum audit proof standard for agents we let through
Avnp-Audit-Minimum: hash-chained

# When verification fails, what to do
Avnp-Challenge-Fallback: captcha

# Sitemap (unchanged)
Sitemap: https://example.com/sitemap.xml
```

### 3. The `.well-known/agent-policy.json` Manifest

The full policy is served as JSON for programmatic consumption (agents parse JSON, not ABNF). `robots.txt` carries the pointer; the JSON carries the semantics.

```typescript
// packages/botauth/src/policy-types.ts
export interface AgentPolicy {
  /** Schema version, currently "1.0" */
  readonly version: "1.0";
  /** Protocol identifier — must match robots.txt Avnp-Protocol */
  readonly protocol: "agentgrid/1.0";
  /** Human-readable site owner name for audit logs */
  readonly siteName: string;
  /** URL of the registry to query for verification */
  readonly registry: string;
  /** Accept-list for operators */
  readonly accept: {
    /** "any-verified" or a list of operator DIDs */
    readonly operators: "any-verified" | readonly `did:${string}:${string}`[];
    /** Required capabilities the agent must hold in its Mandate */
    readonly requiredCapabilities: readonly string[];
    /** Optional: maximum acceptable Mandate expiry window */
    readonly maxMandateTtlSeconds?: number;
  };
  /** Deny-list */
  readonly deny: {
    /** Hard-blocked capabilities even with valid Mandate */
    readonly capabilities: readonly string[];
    /** Hard-blocked agent DIDs */
    readonly agents?: readonly `did:${string}:${string}`[];
    /** User-Agent substrings to block (preserved for legacy bot lists) */
    readonly userAgentSubstrings?: readonly string[];
  };
  /** Per-capability rate limits */
  readonly rateLimits: {
    readonly default: { readonly requests: number; readonly window: "second" | "minute" | "hour" };
    readonly perCapability?: Record<string, { readonly requests: number; readonly window: ... }>;
  };
  /** Minimum audit proof required for admission */
  readonly auditMinimum: "none" | "signed" | "hash-chained" | "external-anchored";
  /** Fallback behavior when agent signature is invalid/missing */
  readonly challengeFallback: "captcha" | "deny" | "step-up";
  /** Optional: hard cap on concurrent active Mandates per operator on this site */
  readonly maxConcurrentMandatesPerOperator?: number;
}
```

**Example served payload:**

```json
{
  "version": "1.0",
  "protocol": "agentgrid/1.0",
  "siteName": "Example SaaS Inc.",
  "registry": "https://registry.agentgrid.io/v1/verify",
  "accept": {
    "operators": "any-verified",
    "requiredCapabilities": ["browse:read", "pay:create"],
    "maxMandateTtlSeconds": 2592000
  },
  "deny": {
    "capabilities": ["*:irreversible", "*:delete", "pay:transfer-third-party"],
    "userAgentSubstrings": ["headless-chrome-?", "mass-downloader/"]
  },
  "rateLimits": {
    "default": { "requests": 60, "window": "minute" },
    "perCapability": {
      "browse:read": { "requests": 600, "window": "minute" },
      "pay:create": { "requests": 10, "window": "minute" }
    }
  },
  "auditMinimum": "hash-chained",
  "challengeFallback": "captcha"
}
```

### 4. The `.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory`

Served by AgentGrid at `https://agents.agentgrid.io/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory?agent={DID}` (per [IETF draft-ietf-httpbis-message-signatures-directory](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-httpbis-message-signatures-directory/)):

```typescript
export interface SignatureDirectory {
  /** AgentGrid-specific extension, anchors agent to operator */
  readonly "agentgrid:operator": `did:${string}:${string}`;
  readonly "agentgrid:displayName": string;
  readonly "agentgrid:status": "active" | "suspended" | "revoked";
  /** Standard key directory payload (RFC-style) */
  readonly keys: ReadonlyArray<{
    readonly kid: string;        // e.g., "did:key:z6Mk...AGENT#key-1"
    readonly kty: "OKP";
    readonly crv: "Ed25519";
    readonly x: string;          // base64url raw public key bytes
    readonly alg: "EdDSA";
    readonly status: "active" | "rotated" | "revoked";
    readonly validFrom: string;  // ISO-8601
    readonly validUntil?: string;
  }>;
  /** Active Mandate references — sites can dereference these to verify authority */
  readonly "agentgrid:mandates": ReadonlyArray<{
    readonly id: string;         // e.g., "mnd_01HXYZ"
    readonly capability: string; // e.g., "browse:read"
    readonly issuer: `did:${string}:${string}`; // operator DID
    readonly endpoint: string;   // URL to fetch full VC
    readonly status: "active" | "expired" | "revoked";
  }>;
}
```

**Example:**

```json
{
  "agentgrid:operator": "did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK",
  "agentgrid:displayName": "Research Agent (Operator: alice@example.com)",
  "agentgrid:status": "active",
  "keys": [
    {
      "kid": "did:key:z6MkiDL6B9yjn4Xx3vFhFDewbnzL2GZKhF7Xm4Gq4XwPb8vJ#key-1",
      "kty": "OKP",
      "crv": "Ed25519",
      "x": "11qYAYKxCrfVS_7TyWQHOg7hcvPapiMlrwIaaPcHURo",
      "alg": "EdDSA",
      "status": "active",
      "validFrom": "2026-06-01T00:00:00Z"
    }
  ],
  "agentgrid:mandates": [
    {
      "id": "mnd_01HXYZABCDEF",
      "capability": "browse:read",
      "issuer": "did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK",
      "endpoint": "https://registry.agentgrid.io/v1/mandates/mnd_01HXYZABCDEF",
      "status": "active"
    }
  ]
}
```

### 5. RFC 9421 Request Signing with AgentGrid Profile

The agent signs every outbound request using RFC 9421 with the `agentgrid-v1` signature tag. Signed components are the request-line-derived elements (authority, path) plus the AgentGrid-typed headers.

```typescript
// packages/botauth/src/signer.ts
import { ed25519 } from "@noble/curves/ed25519";
import { canonicalize } from "json-canonicalize"; // RFC 8785

const SIGNATURE_TAG = "agentgrid-v1";
const DEFAULT_VALIDITY_SECONDS = 60; // tight window to prevent replay

export interface SignedRequestComponents {
  "@authority": string;        // "site.example.com"
  "@path": string;             // "/api/resource"
  "agent-identity": `did:${string}:${string}`;
  "agent-operator": `did:${string}:${string}`;
  "agent-mandate"?: string;    // "mnd_01HXYZABCDEF"
  "agent-capability": string;  // "browse:read"
  "agent-nonce": string;       // 128-bit random
  "agent-timestamp": string;   // ISO-8601 UTC
}

export interface SignedAgentRequest {
  url: string;
  method: string;
  headers: Record<string, string>;
  body?: Uint8Array;
}

export function signAgentRequest(
  request: Omit<SignedAgentRequest, "headers">,
  agent: { did: `did:${string}:${string}`; privateKey: Uint8Array; keyId: string },
  mandate: { id: string; operatorDid: `did:${string}:${string}`; capability: string } | null,
  signer: VaultSigner
): Promise<SignedAgentRequest> {
  const url = new URL(request.url);
  const now = Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000);
  const expires = now + DEFAULT_VALIDITY_SECONDS;
  const nonce = crypto.randomUUID();

  const components: SignedRequestComponents = {
    "@authority": url.host,
    "@path": url.pathname,
    "agent-identity": agent.did,
    "agent-operator": mandate?.operatorDid ?? agent.did.split(":").slice(0, 3).join(":") + "SELF",
    "agent-capability": mandate?.capability ?? "browse:read",
    "agent-nonce": nonce,
    "agent-timestamp": new Date(now * 1000).toISOString(),
  };
  if (mandate) {
    (components as any)["agent-mandate"] = mandate.id;
  }

  // Per RFC 9421 §2.5: serialize as a structured field inner list
  const componentNames = Object.keys(components);
  const signatureInput = 
    `sig1=(${componentNames.map(n => `"${n}"`).join(" ")});` +
    `created=${now};expires=${expires};` +
    `keyid="${agent.keyId}";tag="${SIGNATURE_TAG}"`;

  // JCS-canonicalize the JSON of components (RFC 8785)
  const canonical = canonicalize(components);
  const payload = new TextEncoder().encode(canonical);

  // Sign via the Vault (sign-only, never exposing private key)
  const signature = await signer.sign({ did: agent.did, payload });

  const headers: Record<string, string> = {
    ...request.headers,
    "Signature-Input": signatureInput,
    "Signature": `sig1=:${Buffer.from(signature).toString("base64")}:`,
    "Agent-Identity": components["agent-identity"],
    "Agent-Operator": components["agent-operator"],
    "Agent-Capability": components["agent-capability"],
    "Agent-Nonce": components["agent-nonce"],
    "Agent-Timestamp": components["agent-timestamp"],
  };
  if (mandate) {
    headers["Agent-Mandate"] = mandate.id;
  }
  // Public User-Agent for transparency
  headers["User-Agent"] = `AgentGrid/1.0 (+https://agents.agentgrid.io/identity/${agent.did})`;

  return Promise.resolve({ ...request, headers });
}
```

### 6. Reference Verification Implementation (Cloudflare Worker)

This is the WAF-edge verifier. Written as a Cloudflare Worker, but the same logic ports to AWS Lambda@Edge, Fastly Compute, Akamai EdgeWorkers, or a self-hosted nginx+Lua gateway.

```typescript
// apps/cf-worker-verifier/src/verify.ts
import { ed25519 } from "@noble/curves/ed25519";
import { canonicalize } from "json-canonicalize";

interface VerificationResult {
  allow: boolean;
  decision: "AGENT_VERIFIED" | "AGENT_REJECTED" | "FALLBACK_TO_CAPTCHA" | "RATE_LIMITED";
  reason?: string;
  agentDid?: string;
  operatorDid?: string;
  mandateId?: string;
  capability?: string;
}

export async function verifyAgentRequest(
  request: Request,
  env: { AGENTGRID_REGISTRY: string; POLICY_CACHE: KVNamespace; KEY_CACHE: KVNamespace; MANDATE_CACHE: KVNamespace }
): Promise<VerificationResult> {
  const signatureInput = request.headers.get("Signature-Input");
  const signature = request.headers.get("Signature");
  const agentIdentity = request.headers.get("Agent-Identity");
  const agentOperator = request.headers.get("Agent-Operator");
  const agentMandate = request.headers.get("Agent-Mandate");
  const agentCapability = request.headers.get("Agent-Capability");
  const agentNonce = request.headers.get("Agent-Nonce");
  const agentTimestamp = request.headers.get("Agent-Timestamp");

  // ── STEP 1: Presence checks ───────────────────────────────────────
  if (!signatureInput || !signature || !agentIdentity || !agentOperator || 
      !agentCapability || !agentNonce || !agentTimestamp) {
    return { allow: false, decision: "FALLBACK_TO_CAPTCHA", 
             reason: "missing_agentgrid_headers" };
  }

  // ── STEP 2: Timestamp + replay window check ──────────────────────
  const parsedInput = parseSignatureInput(signatureInput); // RFC 9421 §2.5
  const now = Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000);
  if (parsedInput.expires && parsedInput.expires < now) {
    return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", reason: "signature_expired" };
  }
  if (parsedInput.created && Math.abs(now - parsedInput.created) > 300) {
    return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", reason: "timestamp_skew" };
  }
  // Nonce replay-check via KV (60s rolling window)
  const nonceKey = `nonce:${agentIdentity}:${agentNonce}`;
  const seenNonce = await env.NONCE_CACHE?.get(nonceKey);
  if (seenNonce) {
    return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", reason: "nonce_replay" };
  }
  await env.NONCE_CACHE?.put(nonceKey, "1", { expirationTtl: 120 });

  // ── STEP 3: Fetch + cache site policy (1h TTL) ───────────────────
  const url = new URL(request.url);
  const policy = await getPolicy(url.origin, env.POLICY_CACHE);
  if (!policy) {
    return { allow: false, decision: "FALLBACK_TO_CAPTCHA", 
             reason: "no_policy_published" };
  }

  // ── STEP 4: Fetch + cache key directory (1h TTL) ─────────────────
  const keyDir = await getKeyDirectory(agentIdentity, env.AGENTGRID_REGISTRY, env.KEY_CACHE);
  if (!keyDir || keyDir["agentgrid:status"] !== "active") {
    return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", 
             reason: "agent_revoked_or_inactive" };
  }

  // ── STEP 5: 16.2.2 FIX — Tripartite binding verification ────────
  // 5a. Agent's `Agent-Operator` header MUST equal key directory's operator
  if (keyDir["agentgrid:operator"] !== agentOperator) {
    return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", 
             reason: "agent_operator_binding_mismatch" };
  }
  // 5b. Resolve the key from key directory by kid, NOT from request
  const kid = parsedInput.keyid;
  const keyEntry = keyDir.keys.find(k => k.kid === kid && k.status === "active");
  if (!keyEntry) {
    return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", reason: "unknown_key_id" };
  }
  // 5c. Reconstruct signed components and verify Ed25519 signature
  const components = await extractSignedComponents(request, parsedInput.components);
  const canonical = canonicalize(components);
  const signatureBytes = base64UrlDecode(signature.split(":")[1]);
  const publicKey = base64UrlDecode(keyEntry.x);
  const valid = ed25519.verify(signatureBytes, new TextEncoder().encode(canonical), publicKey);
  if (!valid) {
    return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", reason: "signature_invalid" };
  }

  // ── STEP 6: Mandate resolution + binding ─────────────────────────
  if (policy.accept.requiredCapabilities.length > 0) {
    if (!agentMandate) {
      return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", 
               reason: "mandate_required_for_capability" };
    }
    const mandate = await getMandate(agentMandate, env.AGENTGRID_REGISTRY, env.MANDATE_CACHE);
    if (!mandate || mandate.status !== "active") {
      return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", reason: "mandate_inactive" };
    }
    // 6a. Mandate MUST be issued by the asserted operator (16.2.2 anchor)
    if (mandate.issuer !== agentOperator) {
      return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", 
               reason: "mandate_issuer_mismatch" };
    }
    // 6b. Mandate subject MUST equal the agent identity
    if (mandate.subject !== agentIdentity) {
      return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", 
               reason: "mandate_subject_mismatch" };
    }
    // 6c. Mandate validity window
    if (new Date(mandate.expires) < new Date() || new Date(mandate.notBefore) > new Date()) {
      return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", reason: "mandate_expired_or_not_yet_valid" };
    }
    // 6d. Mandate root must trace to operator (chain validation)
    let rootIssuer = mandate.issuer;
    let cursor = mandate;
    let depth = 0;
    while (cursor.parentId && depth < 10) {
      cursor = await getMandate(cursor.parentId, env.AGENTGRID_REGISTRY, env.MANDATE_CACHE);
      if (!cursor) break;
      rootIssuer = cursor.issuer;
      depth++;
    }
    if (rootIssuer !== agentOperator) {
      return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", 
               reason: "mandate_chain_not_rooted_at_operator" };
    }
  }

  // ── STEP 7: Capability + scope intersection ─────────────────────
  const requiredCaps = policy.accept.requiredCapabilities;
  if (requiredCaps.length > 0 && !requiredCaps.includes(agentCapability)) {
    return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", 
             reason: "capability_not_in_policy_accept" };
  }
  const deniedCaps = policy.deny.capabilities;
  const matchesDeny = deniedCaps.some(pattern => 
    matchCapabilityPattern(pattern, agentCapability));
  if (matchesDeny) {
    return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", 
             reason: "capability_in_policy_deny" };
  }

  // ── STEP 8: Operator allow-list check ────────────────────────────
  if (policy.accept.operators !== "any-verified" && 
      !policy.accept.operators.includes(agentOperator)) {
    return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", 
             reason: "operator_not_in_allowlist" };
  }

  // ── STEP 9: Rate limiting (per operator × capability × site) ────
  const rlKey = `rl:${url.origin}:${agentOperator}:${agentCapability}`;
  const rl = await checkRateLimit(rlKey, policy.rateLimits, env.RATE_LIMITER);
  if (!rl.allowed) {
    return { allow: false, decision: "RATE_LIMITED", reason: "rate_limit_exceeded" };
  }

  // ── STEP 10: Audit minimum standard check ───────────────────────
  if (policy.auditMinimum === "external-anchored" && 
      keyDir["agentgrid:audit"]?.anchor !== "external") {
    return { allow: false, decision: "AGENT_REJECTED", 
             reason: "audit_standard_below_minimum" };
  }

  // ── ALL CHECKS PASSED ──────────────────────────────────────────
  return { 
    allow: true, decision: "AGENT_VERIFIED",
    agentDid: agentIdentity, operatorDid: agentOperator,
    mandateId: agentMandate ?? undefined, capability: agentCapability
  };
}

// Helper: RFC 9421 §2.5 signature-input parser (simplified)
function parseSignatureInput(s: string) {
  const m = s.match(/sig1=\(([^)]+)\);([^;]+(?:;[^;]+)*)/);
  if (!m) throw new Error("malformed Signature-Input");
  const components = m[1].split(/\s+/).map(c => c.replace(/"/g, ""));
  const params: Record<string, string | number> = {};
  for (const p of m[2].split(";")) {
    const [k, v] = p.split("=");
    if (k === "created" || k === "expires") params[k] = parseInt(v, 10);
    else if (k === "keyid") params[k] = v.replace(/"/g, "");
    else if (k === "tag") params[k] = v.replace(/"/g, "");
  }
  return { components, ...params };
}
```

### 7. End-to-End Worked Example

**Step 1 — Site publishes (one-time):**
```bash
$ curl -X PUT https://example.com/.well-known/agent-policy.json -d @policy.json
```
DNS record (optional, for faster discovery): `_agentpolicy.example.com TXT "v=avnp1; uri=https://example.com/.well-known/agent-policy.json"`

**Step 2 — Agent first visit (cold path):**
```http
GET /.well-known/agent-policy.json HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
```
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600
{"version":"1.0","protocol":"agentgrid/1.0",...}
```

**Step 3 — Agent signs request (hot path):**
```http
GET /products/12345 HTTP/1.1
Host: shop.example.com
Signature-Input: sig1=("@authority" "@path" "agent-identity" "agent-operator" "agent-mandate" "agent-capability" "agent-nonce" "agent-timestamp");created=1718280000;expires=1718280060;keyid="did:key:z6MkiDL6B9yjn4Xx3vFhFDewbnzL2GZKhF7Xm4Gq4XwPb8vJ#key-1";tag="agentgrid-v1"
Signature: sig1=:kZ5Vqf2...base64...:
Agent-Identity: did:key:z6MkiDL6B9yjn4Xx3vFhFDewbnzL2GZKhF7Xm4Gq4XwPb8vJ
Agent-Operator: did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK
Agent-Mandate: mnd_01HXYZABCDEF
Agent-Capability: browse:read
Agent-Nonce: 7a3f-9b2e-4c1d
Agent-Timestamp: 2026-06-13T12:00:00Z
User-Agent: AgentGrid/1.0 (+https://agents.agentgrid.io/identity/did:key:z6Mki...)
```

**Step 4 — Site's WAF verifies and forwards:**
```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Agent-Verified: true
Agent-Operator-Verified: true
Agent-Mandate-Scope: browse:read
Agent-Registry-Version: 2026-06-01
X-AgentGrid-Decision-Id: dec_01ABC123
```

**Step 5 — Site logs to its audit log:**
```json
{
  "ts": "2026-06-13T12:00:00.123Z",
  "src_ip": "203.0.113.45",
  "agent_did": "did:key:z6Mki...",
  "operator_did": "did:key:z6Mk...",
  "mandate_id": "mnd_01HXYZABCDEF",
  "capability": "browse:read",
  "decision": "AGENT_VERIFIED",
  "request_path": "/products/12345"
}
```

### 8. Registry Verification Endpoint Contract

The AgentGrid registry exposes a read-only JSON API. Sites cache aggressively; the registry is **not** a single point of failure (mirrored, with anycast or DANE-secured HA endpoints).

```typescript
// GET /v1/verify/agent/{DID}
interface AgentVerificationResponse {
  did: `did:${string}:${string}`;
  operator: `did:${string}:${string}`;
  status: "active" | "suspended" | "revoked";
  publicKeys: ReadonlyArray<{
    kid: string;
    kty: "OKP";
    crv: "Ed25519";
    x: string;
    status: "active" | "rotated" | "revoked";
    validFrom: string;
    validUntil?: string;
  }>;
  statusList: {
    "status-list-2021": {
      uri: string;        // bitstring download for offline StatusList2021
      index: string;
    };
  };
  audit: {
    chainHead: string;     // sha256 of last audit ledger block
    anchor: "none" | "daily-digest" | "external-transparency-log";
  };
  fetchedAt: string;       // ISO-8601, for client-side cache validation
}

// GET /v1/verify/mandate/{mandateId}
interface MandateVerificationResponse {
  id: string;
  issuer: `did:${string}:${string}`;
  subject: `did:${string}:${string}`;
  parentId: string | null;
  capability: string;
  scope: {
    [key: string]: unknown;
  };
  status: "active" | "expired" | "revoked";
  notBefore: string;
  expires: string;
  signature: string;        // base64 JCS-signed proof
  proofPurpose: "assertionMethod";
  verificationMethod: `did:${string}:${string}`;  // resolves to operator's key
}
```

### 9. WAF / CDN Integration Patterns

| Platform | Implementation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Cloudflare** | Worker (`@cloudflare/workers-types`), KV for policy/cache | Per-Worker route binding; `request.headers` access; `cache.put()` for nonce dedup |
| **AWS CloudFront** | Lambda@Edge (viewer-request) | Limited to 1MB response; pre-compile the policy to bundle |
| **Fastly** | Compute@Edge (Rust/Wasm) | Sub-ms verification; `Edge.acl` for nonce dedup |
| **Akamai** | EdgeWorkers | JavaScript; ObjectStore for cache |
| **Self-hosted nginx** | `nginx-mod-secure-link` + Lua (OpenResty) | Local Redis for nonce/rate-limit |
| **Vercel/Next.js Middleware** | Edge Middleware (`middleware.ts`) | Easy for SaaS apps; hits 25ms cold-start ceiling |
| **Express middleware** | `express-agentgrid` npm package | Drop-in for any Node HTTP server; full-featured, slower than edge |

### 10. Operator-Facing SDK (TypeScript)

```typescript
// packages/executor/src/agent-client.ts
import { signAgentRequest } from "@agentgrid/botauth";
import { VaultSigner } from "@agentgrid/vault";

export class AgentHttpClient {
  constructor(
    private readonly vault: VaultSigner,
    private readonly registry: string,
    private readonly policyCache: Map<string, AgentPolicy> = new Map()
  ) {}

  async fetch(
    url: string, 
    init: RequestInit & { 
      agent: { did: `did:${string}:${string}`; keyId: string };
      mandate?: { id: string; operatorDid: `did:${string}:${string}`; capability: string };
    }
  ): Promise<Response> {
    const parsed = new URL(url);
    
    // Phase 0: policy discovery (cached)
    let policy = this.policyCache.get(parsed.origin);
    if (!policy) {
      const res = await fetch(`${parsed.origin}/.well-known/agent-policy.json`);
      if (res.ok) {
        policy = await res.json();
        this.policyCache.set(parsed.origin, policy!);
      }
    }

    // Phase 2: sign and send
    const signed = await signAgentRequest(
      { url, method: init.method ?? "GET", body: init.body as Uint8Array },
      { ...init.agent, privateKey: new Uint8Array() },  // privateKey ignored — Vault signs
      init.mandate ?? null,
      this.vault
    );
    return fetch(signed.url, { 
      method: signed.method, 
      headers: signed.headers, 
      body: signed.body 
    });
  }
}
```

---

## ⚠️ FAILURE MODES & TRUST HARDENING

### Threat 1 — Identity Spoofing (Cure: 16.2.2 Tripartite Binding)

**Attack:** Attacker generates a `did:key` for a fake agent, signs a request, claims `Agent-Operator: did:key:z6Mk...ALICE` where Alice is a real, verified operator.

**Failure mode (pre-AVANP):** Server verifies the request signature against the agent's DID and accepts. No link check between agent and operator.

**Mitigation (in spec):** Steps 5a/5b/6a/6b/6d of the verifier enforce the tripartite binding. The agent's DID is resolved via the key directory → the directory's pinned `agentgrid:operator` field MUST equal the `Agent-Operator` header. The Mandate's `issuer` MUST equal the `Agent-Operator` header. The Mandate's `subject` MUST equal the `Agent-Identity` header. The root Mandate in the chain MUST trace back to the asserted operator. Any mismatch → `AGENT_REJECTED` with explicit reason code.

**Audit emit:** Every rejection includes a `reason` field, logged to AgentGrid's `audit_ledger` with `event_type = "negotiation_rejected"`, allowing operators to detect spoofing attempts against their identity.

### Threat 2 — Signature Replay (Cure: Timestamp + Nonce + Cache)

**Attack:** Attacker captures a valid signed request, replays it 1000× within seconds to exhaust the operator's rate-limit budget or trigger duplicate payments.

**Mitigation:**
1. **60-second `expires` window** on the `Signature-Input` (Step 2 of verifier).
2. **`@nbf`-style `created` validation** with ±5 minute skew tolerance, then strict reject.
3. **Nonce dedup at the edge** via KV/Redis (120s TTL). Replayed nonce → `nonce_replay` rejection.
4. **Mandate-level idempotency keys** for mutating requests (POST/PUT/DELETE) — the agent includes a `Mandate-Nonce` header (separate from the signature nonce); the server caches it for the Mandate's TTL and rejects duplicates with HTTP 409.

### Threat 3 — Key Compromise (Cure: Short-Lived Validity + Rapid Rotation)

**Attack:** Adversary exfiltrates the agent's private key (e.g., via a vulnerable Vault, or by compromising the host process).

**Mitigation:**
1. **60-second signature validity** limits the window of any captured signature.
2. **Ed25519 keys are pinned to a single agent**; the operator cannot reuse them.
3. **Key rotation** is performed via the `EncryptedFileVaultSigner` flow from §16.1.4 — old key gets `status: "rotated"`, new key is published, signatures using the old key stop being accepted immediately (the key directory caches a 1h TTL but checks the key's `status` field, which the registry updates within seconds of rotation).
4. **Operator kill switch** — revoking the agent's Passport in the registry causes the next `getKeyDirectory` call to return `status: "revoked"`, blocking all future signed requests (within cache TTL of 1 hour; for high-security operators, KV cache TTL can be tuned to 60s).
5. **Vault tier upgrade** — the production seam (§16.4) moves the private key to KMS/HSM, so the key is never present in process memory in raw form.

### Threat 4 — Mandate Escalation (Cure: Chain Validation + Scope Intersection)

**Attack:** Compromised agent, given a `browse:read` Mandate, attempts to claim `pay:create` capability by forging a Mandate header.

**Mitigation:**
1. The site fetches the asserted Mandate by ID and verifies `issuer == Agent-Operator` (Step 6a).
2. The site confirms `subject == Agent-Identity` (Step 6b).
3. The site fetches the full Mandate VC and confirms the asserted `capability` is in `scope.capabilities` (Step 7).
4. The site may also enforce `capability ⊆ policy.accept.requiredCapabilities` (Step 7).
5. The Mandate VC's `proof` (an Ed25519 signature by the operator over the JCS-canonicalized VC) is verified against the operator's `did:key` resolved from the operator's `http-message-signatures-directory` endpoint. The operator's public key cannot be self-asserted by the agent because the agent does not control the operator's private key.

### Threat 5 — Registry Compromise / Unavailability

**Attack:** Adversary compromises the AgentGrid registry to issue fake "active" statuses for malicious agents, or DDoSes the registry so legitimate agents can't be verified.

**Mitigation:**
1. **Defense in depth — `did:key` is self-certifying.** Even if the registry is down, the cryptographic signature on the request still verifies against the public key embedded in the agent's DID. Sites can run in a **degraded mode** where they verify signatures + maintain a local key cache (1h+TTL) and skip the registry check; this preserves authentication but loses revocation freshness. The `Avnp-Audit-Minimum: hash-chained` directive lets sites opt out of registry-requiring audit checks.
2. **Registry decentralization.** The registry is mirrored across ≥3 geographic regions with anycast DNS; site operators can pin to a specific jurisdiction (`registry.us.agentgrid.io`, `registry.eu.agentgrid.io`).
3. **Local trust cache.** Sites cache `(DID → publicKey, status)` tuples aggressively; only a full registry outage of >1h breaks verification. Sites that need higher freshness run a local **registry replicator** that polls every 60s and serves from a local KV/Redis.
4. **DNSSEC + DANE** on registry endpoints to prevent MITM.

### Threat 6 — Monoculture Attack on Ed25519

**Attack:** A future cryptanalytic break of Ed25519 would compromise every AgentGrid-signed request.

**Mitigation:**
1. **Algorithm agility via the `alg` field in the key directory.** Switching to Ed448, Dilithium (post-quantum), or hybrid signatures is a registry-side update + agent-side config change; no protocol rewrite required.
2. **Post-quantum roadmap** — when NIST PQC standards stabilize (FIPS 203/204/205), AgentGrid transitions to a hybrid Ed25519+ML-DSA scheme for new Passports, with a 5-year sunset on pure-Ed25519 signatures.

### Threat 7 — Privacy Leakage of Operator Identity

**Attack:** Sites receive `Agent-Operator: did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK` — they can correlate all of an operator's agent activity across the web, building a behavioral profile.

**Mitigation:**
1. **Operator pseudonyms.** An operator can mint *per-site* sub-DIDs, each with its own keypair, by issuing narrow sub-Mandates. The site's WAF only ever sees the sub-DID; cross-site correlation requires the operator's consent (a `correlation: opt-in` field in the Mandate).
2. **Optional zero-knowledge disclosure.** Future work: integrate BBS+ signatures for selective disclosure, where the agent proves "I hold a Mandate from a verified operator" without revealing which operator.
3. **Per-site operator allowlists** (the `Avnp-Accept-Operators` directive) reduce the data set the site has to process to a known set.

### Threat 8 — CAPTCHA Fallback Abuse (Cure: Bounded STEP_UP)

**Attack:** Attacker, unable to forge a valid signed request, intentionally sends malformed `Signature` headers to force `FALLBACK_TO_CAPTCHA`, then uses a CAPTCHA-solving farm to bypass.

**Mitigation:**
1. **`Avnp-Challenge-Fallback`** directive lets the site choose: `captcha` (standard), `deny` (hard block unsigned), or **`step-up`** (push notification to a human, who must approve via a signed envelope — see §7 of the Blueprint).
2. **CAPTCHA farms are rate-limited** by IP/ASN at the WAF level, separate from the AgentGrid rate limit.
3. **Operator-side response** — if an operator sees in their dashboard that 100% of their agent's requests are falling back to CAPTCHA, the agent's key is likely compromised, and they hit the kill switch.
4. **STEP_UP fallback is the production gold standard** — for sites that opt in, the user receives a 5-second push notification "Approve agent's access to example.com?" → on-device biometric → signed approval returned → forwarded.

### Threat 9 — Mandate Reference Leakage

**Attack:** Adversary scrapes `Agent-Mandate: mnd_01HXYZABCDEF` from network traffic, then replays the Mandate ID to impersonate the agent on a different site.

**Mitigation:**
1. **Mandate ID is opaque** and meaningless without the corresponding `Agent-Identity` (which is bound to the signature, which is bound to the private key).
2. **Mandate IDs are scoped** — a Mandate can include a `site_constraint` field that limits the Mandate to a specific origin or origin-pattern. Cross-site use of a Mandate ID is rejected by the site's verifier.
3. **Optional short-lived bearer Mandates** — for high-value operations, the operator's approval app can mint a single-use Mandate (TTL: 30 seconds) tied to a specific URL.

### Threat 10 — Timing Side Channels in Verifier

**Attack:** Verifier leaks information about Mandate validity through differing response times (`valid Mandate: 80ms` vs. `expired Mandate: 20ms`).

**Mitigation:**
1. **Constant-time verification** — the verifier pads its response time to a fixed window (e.g., 100ms ± 10ms jitter) before returning. Implementable via `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 100))` after the decision is made.
2. **Uniform error responses** — every rejection returns the same response shape, status code, and timing. Error reasons are returned only in a signed `Agent-Grid-Decision-Receipt` header that the operator can decrypt.

### Threat 11 — Site Policy Impersonation (Cache Poisoning)

**Attack:** Adversary MITM the `.well-known/agent-policy.json` fetch on the agent's first visit, serving a malicious policy that whitelists malicious agents.

**Mitigation:**
1. **TLS 1.3 mandatory** for all `.well-known` fetches.
2. **Optional policy signing** — sites MAY sign their policy with a published site key (`Avnp-Policy-Signature` HTTP header), and agents cache the site public key via a `TLSA` DANE record. Future: bind the policy signature to a CA-issued certificate via RFC 9162 Certificate Transparency.

### Rate-Limiting and Revocation Architecture

```sql
-- Cloudflare D1 / Postgres schema for the site's verifier backend
CREATE TABLE rate_limit_buckets (
  bucket_key   TEXT PRIMARY KEY,        -- "site:operator_did:capability"
  window_start TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  request_count INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  blocked_until TIMESTAMPTZ,
  updated_at    TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);
CREATE INDEX idx_rl_window ON rate_limit_buckets(window_start);

CREATE TABLE agent_audit_log (
  id            BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  ts            TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  agent_did     TEXT NOT NULL,
  operator_did  TEXT NOT NULL,
  mandate_id    TEXT,
  capability    TEXT,
  decision      TEXT NOT NULL,           -- AGENT_VERIFIED|REJECTED|RATE_LIMITED
  reason        TEXT,
  request_host  TEXT NOT NULL,
  request_path  TEXT,
  src_ip        INET,
  signature_nonce TEXT
);
CREATE INDEX idx_audit_operator ON agent_audit_log(operator_did, ts);
CREATE INDEX idx_audit_agent ON agent_audit_log(agent_did, ts);

-- Revocation propagation: site periodically pulls AgentGrid StatusList2021
CREATE TABLE revocation_cache (
  did             TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  status          TEXT NOT NULL,          -- active|revoked|suspended
  last_checked    TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  next_check_at   TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  source          TEXT                     -- agentgrid|web-beth|local
);
CREATE INDEX idx_revoc_next ON revocation_cache(next_check_at);
```

**Revocation propagation SLAs:**
- Agent-initiated revocation → propagated to all site KV caches within **60 seconds** (sites poll `https://registry.agentgrid.io/v1/status/{DID}` every 60s; high-value sites every 10s).
- Operator-initiated kill switch → same path, additionally fires a `Revocation-Broadcast` SSE event to subscribed sites for sub-10s propagation.

### Fallback Decision Matrix (Final)

| Site config (`Avnp-Challenge-Fallback`) | Agent behavior when negotiation fails | Human impact |
|---|---|---|
| `captcha` | Submit to standard bot detection; defer to Turnstile/hCaptcha | ~5s friction per hard wall |
| `deny` | Return 403 to executor; executor surfaces "blocked by site policy" to LLM | None; agent tries alternate path |
| `step-up` | Push notification to operator's phone with deep-link to approve/reject | One tap per site first-visit |
| _(unspecified, default)_ | `captcha` (back-compat with current web) | Same as `captcha` |

### Summary of the Anti-16.2.2 Properties

| Property | 16.2.2 Vulnerability | AVANP Cure |
|---|---|---|
| **Key-identifier binding** | Self-asserted `proof.verificationMethod` | `kid` resolved from key directory, not from request |
| **Agent-operator binding** | Not enforced | Header `Agent-Operator` MUST match key directory's pinned operator |
| **Mandate root binding** | Issuer could be any key | Mandate `issuer` MUST match asserted `Agent-Operator`; chain root MUST match |
| **Mandate subject binding** | Not enforced | Mandate `subject` MUST match `Agent-Identity` header |
| **Key compromise window** | Unlimited (long-lived tokens) | 60-second signature expiry + nonced replay-cache |
| **Registry as single point of failure** | Hard dependency on registry | `did:key` self-certification + cached key directory (1h TTL) enables degraded mode |

This protocol makes "the agent is safe and is being used by this user" **cryptographically assertable to a random site** in under 50ms, with three independent binding checks (request→key, key→operator, operator→Mandate) that make impersonation computationally infeasible, and a graceful fallback path for the sites that haven't yet adopted it.

---

## 📊 IDENTITY & PROTOCOL DIAGNOSIS

### The Core Wedge: RFC 9421 HTTP Message Signatures + Cloudflare Web Bot Auth

The mechanism by which AgentGrid communicates "this is an accountable, verified agent operated by a real human" to a remote site is **Cloudflare's Web Bot Auth** (WBA) framework, which is an application of **IETF RFC 9421 HTTP Message Signatures** over an agent's Passport keypair. This is fundamentally different from—and explicitly opposed to—CAPTCHA solver exploits, anti-detect browser fingerprinting, headless browser masquerade, or any other form of deception-based evasion. The agent declares itself, cryptographically, and the site either honors that declaration or doesn't.

The chain of trust is:

1. **Operator → Passport binding (offline, human-controlled):** A human operator generates an Ed25519 keypair on a trusted device (Secure Enclave / Keychain / KMS), signs a Passport document binding that key to their own operator DID, and publishes the operator's public key material at a `.well-known` directory under a domain they control. The Passport includes a `serviceEndpoints.botAuthKeys` pointer to the directory.

2. **Passport → Agent identity (signed by operator):** The operator signs the agent's Passport with their operator key, binding the agent's DID to the operator's DID. This is the trust root: the Passport `proof` carries the operator's signature, verified against the operator's well-known key.

3. **Agent → HTTP request signature (per-request, automatic):** When the agent's browser or HTTP executor makes an outbound request, the executor's RFC 9421 signer constructs a `Signature-Input` header listing the signed components (at minimum `@authority`, `@path`, and a custom `signature-agent` directive), computes a JCS-canonical signature base, and signs it with the agent's private key via the Vault's sign-only API. The signature is then placed in the `Signature` header. The agent's key bytes **never** leave the Vault boundary.

4. **Remote site → Registry lookup + signature verification (online, site-driven):** The site (or its CDN/WAF) extracts the `signature-agent` directive, fetches the agent's key directory, verifies the public key is anchored to a `did:key` whose `verificationMethod` matches the signing key, checks the Passport's operator-binding signature against the operator's published key, validates the HTTP request signature against the agent's public key, and applies a local policy decision: **allow silently, allow with rate cap, challenge via Turnstile, or block.**

### Why This Beats Solver Exploits

| Property | Solver Exploit Path | Signed-Agent Path |
|---|---|---|
| Arms race | Continuous (Turing tests get harder) | Stable (cryptography is monotonic) |
| Detection-evasion cost | Browser fingerprint spoofing, canvas/audio randomizers, residential proxy rotation, mouse-curve simulation — all degrade in maintenance | None — agent declares itself |
| Liability on misuse | Solver operators are ATO enablers; abuse is structural | Agent identity is non-repudiable; abuse traces to a signed operator |
| CAPTCHA vendor ToS | 2Captcha, Anti-Captcha, etc. are explicitly ToS violations for the protecting site | Honored by Cloudflare, Fastly, Akamai, Visa, Mastercard, Stripe |
| Moral posture | Adversarial to the site | Cooperative with the site |

Cloudflare's `web-bot-auth` library (`@cloudflare/web-bot-auth`) is the reference implementation; the agent registers in Cloudflare's `bot-directory` or runs an equivalent at its own `.well-known` endpoint, and the verifying CDN checks the signature on every request and skips the interactive challenge for verified agents according to site policy.

### Cryptographic Primitives in Use

- **Signing algorithm:** Ed25519 (RFC 8032) via `@noble/ed25519`. Ed25519 is the IETF-recommended 2026 default for HTTP message signatures — deterministic (no RNG failure mode), compact (64-byte signatures), fast (~0.1ms verify), and now widely supported by WAFs.
- **Canonicalization:** JCS (RFC 8785) for any JSON payload, plus RFC 9421's `httpbis-1` component normalization for the HTTP signature base. Both are deterministic across implementations; this is critical because the verifier on the server side must reproduce the exact same byte string the signer produced, or verification fails.
- **DID method:** `did:key` with multibase `z6Mk…` encoding (multicodec `0xed01` for Ed25519). The DID is the public key — no resolution lookup needed for the base case. Operator binding uses `did:web` (e.g., `did:web:agents.yourdomain.com:operator-jane`) so the operator can rotate keys without changing the identifier.
- **Verifiable Credentials:** AgentGrid issues a W3C VC 2.0 `AgentIdentityCredential` whose `credentialSubject.id` is the agent's DID, with claims about the agent's `operator`, `publicKey`, and `serviceEndpoints`. Sites that support VC verification (using `did-jwt-vc` or `@veramo/core`) can resolve the chain themselves; sites that don't can defer to a registry lookup.

### Turnstile-Specific Integration

Cloudflare Turnstile is the canonical 2026 WAF challenge: a managed challenge that issues a `cf-turnstile-token` after running a non-interactive proof-of-work and behavioral analysis. **Critically, Turnstile does not block signed agents by default** — Cloudflare's customer dashboard exposes a "Verified Bots" allowlist, and once an agent's `signature-agent` is in the bot directory, Turnstile issues a silent pass (no token required) for matching requests. The site's `cf.bot_management` field in the request log records `bot_score: 99, verified_bot: true, verified_bot_name: "AgentGrid:agent-research"`.

The protocol does **not** require Turnstile to be bypassed via automation. The agent does not solve a CAPTCHA. The agent does not run a headless browser and pray. The agent signs the request, and the WAF recognizes the signature and serves the response.

---

## 🛠️ DETAILED TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION

### 3.1 The RFC 9421 Signature Construction

For a request like `POST https://api.acme-corp.com/v1/payees`, the executor constructs the following headers:

```
POST /v1/payees HTTP/1.1
Host: api.acme-corp.com
Content-Type: application/json
User-Agent: AgentGrid/0.4.0 (agent-did:key:z6Mki…ABC; operator-did:web:agents.yourdomain.com:operator-jane)
Content-Digest: sha-256=:X48E9qOokqqrvdts8nOJRJN3OWDUoyWxBf7kbu9DBPE=:
Signature-Input: sig1=("@authority" "@path" "@method" "content-type" "content-digest" "signature-agent");created=1731600000;expires=1731600060;nonce="a3b9c7e1f4d2b8a5";keyid="did:key:z6Mki…ABC#z6Mki…ABC"
Signature: sig1=:pG3nJp1y…base64-signature-bytes…:
signature-agent: sig1=https://agents.yourdomain.com/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory/agent-research
```

#### Signature Base Computation (RFC 9421 §2.5)

The verifier reconstructs the signature base as follows. Implement this in TypeScript using the `http-message-sigatures` npm package, which Cloudflare maintains as the reference:

```typescript
import { createSignatureBase, createSignature } from "@cloudflare/web-bot-auth";
import { VaultSigner } from "@AgentGrid/vault";

export class HttpRequestSigner {
  constructor(
    private readonly vault: VaultSigner,
    private readonly agentDid: string,
    private readonly signatureAgentUri: string
  ) {}

  async signRequest(req: {
    method: string;
    url: string;
    headers: Record<string, string>;
    body?: Uint8Array;
  }): Promise<{ signedHeaders: Record<string, string> }> {
    // 1. Compute content-digest (SHA-256 of body) per RFC 9530
    const bodyHash = req.body
      ? "sha-256=:" + toBase64(sha256(req.body)) + ":"
      : undefined;

    const componentsToSign = [
      "@authority",
      "@path",
      "@method",
      "content-type",
      ...(bodyHash ? ["content-digest"] : []),
      "signature-agent",
    ];

    // 2. Build the signature base per RFC 9421 §2.5
    const created = Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000);
    const expires = created + 60; // 60-second replay window
    const nonce = crypto.randomBytes(8).toString("hex");

    const sigInput = `sig1=(${componentsToSign.map(c => `"${c}"`).join(" ")});created=${created};expires=${expires};nonce="${nonce}";keyid="${this.agentDid}#${this.agentDid.split(":key:")[1]}"`;

    const signatureBase = createSignatureBase({
      method: req.method,
      url: req.url,
      headers: {
        ...req.headers,
        "content-digest": bodyHash,
        "signature-agent": this.signatureAgentUri,
      },
      components: componentsToSign,
      signatureParams: sigInput,
    });

    // 3. Sign via Vault (private key never leaves HSM)
    const signatureBytes = await this.vault.sign({
      did: this.agentDid,
      payload: new TextEncoder().encode(signatureBase),
    });

    // 4. Build the Signature header value
    const signature = `sig1=:${toBase64Url(signatureBytes)}:`;

    return {
      signedHeaders: {
        ...req.headers,
        "content-digest": bodyHash ?? req.headers["content-digest"],
        "signature-agent": this.signatureAgentUri,
        "signature-input": sigInput,
        signature,
      },
    };
  }
}
```

#### Verifier Side (Reference Implementation for Sites)

A site that wants to honor AgentGrid-signed agents implements the inverse. Below is a production-grade verifier suitable for deployment as a Cloudflare Worker or edge function:

```typescript
import { verifySignature, SignatureDirectory } from "@cloudflare/web-bot-auth";
import { Resolver } from "did-resolver";
import { getResolver as getKeyResolver } from "did-key";

export class SignedAgentVerifier {
  private readonly directoryCache = new Map<string, CachedDirectory>();
  private readonly resolver: Resolver;

  constructor(private readonly directoryUrl: string) {
    this.resolver = new Resolver({
      ...getKeyResolver(),
      // did:web resolver registered here; omitted for brevity
    });
  }

  async verify(req: {
    method: string;
    url: string;
    headers: Record<string, string>;
    body?: Uint8Array;
  }): Promise<VerificationResult> {
    // 1. Extract signature-agent directive
    const sigInput = req.headers["signature-input"];
    if (!sigInput) return { ok: false, reason: "no_signature" };
    const parsed = parseSignatureInput(sigInput);
    const sigAgentHeader = req.headers["signature-agent"];
    if (!sigAgentHeader) return { ok: false, reason: "no_signature_agent" };

    // 2. Resolve the directory and find the agent's public key
    const sigAgentUrl = extractSigAgentUrl(sigAgentHeader);
    const directory = await this.fetchDirectory(sigAgentUrl);
    const keyIdMatch = parsed.keyid;
    if (!keyIdMatch) return { ok: false, reason: "no_keyid" };

    // 3. Locate the public key in the directory
    const publicKey = directory.keys?.[keyIdMatch];
    if (!publicKey) return { ok: false, reason: "key_not_found" };

    // 4. Verify the HTTP signature
    const valid = await verifySignature({
      method: req.method,
      url: req.url,
      headers: req.headers,
      components: parsed.components,
      signatureParams: sigInput,
      signature: req.headers["signature"],
      publicKey: await importJWK(publicKey.jwk),
    });
    if (!valid) return { ok: false, reason: "bad_signature" };

    // 5. Check freshness (60s window prevents replay)
    const now = Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000);
    if (parsed.expires && now > parsed.expires)
      return { ok: false, reason: "expired" };
    if (parsed.created && now - parsed.created > 300)
      return { ok: false, reason: "stale" };

    // 6. Resolve the agent's DID and verify operator binding
    //    (this is the 16.2.2 mitigation: the operator signature must
    //    be over a passport that names this exact verificationMethod)
    const agentDoc = await this.resolver.resolve(publicKey.controller);
    if (!agentDoc.didDocument) return { ok: false, reason: "did_unresolved" };
    const passport = await this.fetchPassport(publicKey.controller);
    const operatorKey = await this.fetchOperatorKey(passport.operator);

    // 7. Verify the Passport's proof using ONLY the operator's declared key
    //    (16.2.2 fix: do not trust the passport's claim about its own
    //    verification method — trust the operator's well-known directory)
    const passportSigValid = await this.verifyWithOperatorKey(
      passport,
      operatorKey
    );
    if (!passportSigValid)
      return { ok: false, reason: "passport_not_operator_signed" };

    // 8. Confirm the agent's signing key matches the passport's binding
    //    (defense-in-depth against 16.2.1 root-trust bypass)
    if (passport.publicKey.jwk.x !== publicKey.jwk.x)
      return { ok: false, reason: "key_passport_mismatch" };

    // 9. Optional: check revocation via registry
    const status = await this.checkRevocation(publicKey.controller);
    if (status === "revoked") return { ok: false, reason: "revoked" };

    return {
      ok: true,
      agentDid: publicKey.controller,
      operator: passport.operator,
      directoryEntry: directory,
    };
  }
}
```

### 3.2 The `.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory` Schema

Each agent publishes a directory at a URL referenced from its Passport. The format follows IETF draft-ietf-httpbis-message-signatures-directory:

```json
{
  "directory_version": 1,
  "agent_id": "did:key:z6MkiTLxJjYoWNcuCqbjxr7PgxQ9e3HJBZHYM2qkB2EKAxR8",
  "operator": "did:web:agents.yourdomain.com:operator-jane",
  "display_name": "AgentGrid Research Agent",
  "contact": "mailto:[email protected]",
  "capabilities": ["browse", "comms", "pay", "deploy"],
  "keys": {
    "did:key:z6MkiTLxJjYoWNcuCqbjxr7PgxQ9e3HJBZHYM2qkB2EKAxR8#z6MkiTLxJjYoWNcuCqbjxr7PgxQ9e3HJBZHYM2qkB2EKAxR8": {
      "status": "active",
      "jwk": {
        "kty": "OKP",
        "crv": "Ed25519",
        "kid": "z6MkiTLxJjYoWNcuCqbjxr7PgxQ9e3HJBZHYM2qkB2EKAxR8",
        "x": "zUC7G3…base64url-public-key…"
      },
      "valid_from": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
      "valid_until": "2027-06-12T10:00:00Z"
    }
  },
  "metadata": {
    "audit_log": "https://agents.yourdomain.com/audit/z6MkiTLx…",
    "agent_brand": "AgentGrid",
    "policy_url": "https://agentgrid.dev/policy/agent-conduct"
  }
}
```

The directory is hosted at a stable HTTPS endpoint. The `signature-agent` header references a *directory URL* (not a key URL), and the verifier walks the directory to find the key referenced by the signature's `keyid` parameter.

### 3.3 The Passport Document (Operator-Signed Identity Anchor)

```jsonc
{
  "@context": [
    "https://www.w3.org/ns/credentials/v2",
    "https://agentgrid.dev/ns/passport/v1"
  ],
  "type": ["VerifiableCredential", "AgentPassport"],
  "id": "urn:uuid:9b1deb4d-3b7d-4bad-9bdd-2b0d7b3dcb6d",
  "issuer": "did:web:agents.yourdomain.com:operator-jane",
  "validFrom": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
  "validUntil": "2027-06-12T10:00:00Z",
  "credentialSubject": {
    "id": "did:key:z6MkiTLxJjYoWNcuCqbjxr7PgxQ9e3HJBZHYM2qkB2EKAxR8",
    "agentName": "Research Agent",
    "agentType": "delegate",
    "publicKey": {
      "id": "did:key:z6MkiTLxJjYoWNcuCqbjxr7PgxQ9e3HJBZHYM2qkB2EKAxR8#z6MkiTLxJjYoWNcuCqbjxr7PgxQ9e3HJBZHYM2qkB2EKAxR8",
      "type": "Ed25519VerificationKey2020",
      "publicKeyJwk": {
        "kty": "OKP",
        "crv": "Ed25519",
        "x": "zUC7G3…"
      }
    },
    "serviceEndpoints": {
      "botAuthKeys": "https://agents.yourdomain.com/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory/agent-research",
      "auditLog": "https://agents.yourdomain.com/audit/z6MkiTLx…"
    },
    "claims": {
      "operatedBy": "did:web:agents.yourdomain.com:operator-jane",
      "jurisdiction": "US-CA",
      "dataResidency": "US",
      "humanInTheLoop": true,
      "scope": "research-and-procurement"
    }
  },
  "proof": {
    "type": "Ed25519Signature2020",
    "verificationMethod": "did:web:agents.yourdomain.com:operator-jane#z6MkjF8…",
    "created": "2026-06-12T10:00:00Z",
    "proofPurpose": "assertionMethod",
    "jws": "eyJhbGciOiJFZERTQSIsImNyaXQiOlsiYjY0Il0sImI2NCI6ZmFsc2V9..b2jRf2zE4w…"
  }
}
```

The `proof.jws` is a signature over the JCS canonicalization (RFC 8785) of the credential body with the `proof` field stripped. The `verificationMethod` is **the operator's own key**, not the agent's. This is the 16.2.2 fix enforced: the verifier extracts the verification key from the operator's `.well-known/did.json` (for `did:web`) and uses that to verify the proof — never the verification method the credential *claims* to use.

### 3.4 Cloudflare WAF Configuration (Site-Side)

A site that wants to honor AgentGrid-signed agents configures Cloudflare as follows. This is a Bot Management configuration, not a Turnstile bypass — Turnstile simply never fires:

```
# In Cloudflare Dashboard → Security → Bots → Verified Bots
# Add: "AgentGrid Agent" → directory: https://agents.yourdomain.com/.well-known/http-message-signatures-directory
# Static resource protection: OFF (allow silent pass)
# Definitely automated: Static resource protection: ALLOW
# Verified bots: ALLOW (skip Turnstile)
# Likely automated: CHALLENGE
# Likely human: ALLOW
```

The corresponding Terraform:

```hcl
resource "cloudflare_bot_management" "agentgrid_policy" {
  zone_id = var.zone_id
  enable_js = true
  rules {
    name      = "Allow Verified AgentGrid Agents"
    condition = "cf.verified_bot and cf.verified_bot_name eq 'AgentGrid'"
    action    = "allow"
  }
  rules {
    name      = "Rate-Cap Unknown Agents"
    condition = "not cf.verified_bot and ip.src in {bad_ranges}"
    action    = "challenge"
    rate_limit = 5
    period    = 60
  }
}
```

When a signed request arrives, Cloudflare sets `cf.verified_bot: true` and `cf.verified_bot_name: "AgentGrid:research-agent"` in the request log, and the WAF skips the Turnstile interactive challenge. The site serves the response without a `cf-turnstile-token` ever being issued.

### 3.5 The Executor Wiring (Agent-Side)

The Playwright/Chromium driver is configured with a request interceptor that signs every outbound request automatically:

```typescript
// packages/primitives/browser/src/signed-request-interceptor.ts
import { Page, Request } from "playwright";
import { HttpRequestSigner } from "@AgentGrid/botauth";

export class SignedRequestInterceptor {
  constructor(
    private readonly signer: HttpRequestSigner,
    private readonly registry: AgentRegistry
  ) {}

  async attach(page: Page): Promise<void> {
    await page.route("**/*", async (route) => {
      const request = route.request();
      const url = new URL(request.url());

      // 1. Determine if the target site honors signed-agent identity
      const sitePolicy = await this.registry.getSitePolicy(url.host);

      if (sitePolicy.signingRequired || sitePolicy.signingRecognized) {
        // 2. Reconstruct the request with signature headers
        const signed = await this.signer.signRequest({
          method: request.method(),
          url: request.url(),
          headers: request.headers(),
          body: request.postDataBuffer() ?? undefined,
        });

        // 3. Forward with the signature added
        await route.continue({
          headers: { ...request.headers(), ...signed.signedHeaders },
        });
      } else {
        // 4. Unsigned site: log a policy decision and pass through
        await this.audit.log({
          type: "signed_request_skipped",
          url: url.host,
          reason: "site_does_not_honor_signing",
        });
        await route.continue();
      }
    });
  }
}
```

For pure HTTP API calls (non-browser), the executor's `fetch` polyfill wraps `undici` with the same signer. The agent itself never sees this code; the executor's tool surface abstracts it as `browse.navigate(url)` or `comms.send(...)`.

### 3.6 Turnstile Fallback (When a Site Doesn't Honor Signing)

When the site has not yet onboarded Web Bot Auth — which in 2026 is still the majority of sites — the WAF issues a Turnstile challenge. AgentGrid's stance is explicit: **do not build a Turnstile solver, do not integrate with 2Captcha, do not use any anti-detect browser trick to slip past.** The design rule from the blueprint §6 holds.

Instead, the executor detects the Turnstile interstitial (HTTP 403 with `cf-mitigated: challenge` header, or a page containing `cf-turnstile`) and routes the request through the **STEP_UP fallback**:

```typescript
export class TurnstileFallback {
  async handleChallenge(
    page: Page,
    challenge: Challenge
  ): Promise<Resolution> {
    // 1. Verify the challenge is real Cloudflare Turnstile, not a phishing page
    const isReal = await this.verifyChallengeSite(challenge.url);
    if (!isReal) {
      throw new PolicyError("fake_challenge_detected", {
        message: "Possible phishing page masquerading as Cloudflare challenge",
      });
    }

    // 2. Emit a STEP_UP audit event
    const approvalId = await this.approval.createRequest({
      type: "captcha_solve_required",
      url: challenge.url,
      reason: "Site does not honor RFC 9421 signed-agent identity",
      ttlMs: 60_000,
    });

    // 3. Block the agent's task on the approval channel
    //    (one push to the operator's phone, not a tap on every action)
    return { status: "blocked", approvalId, action: "await_human" };
  }
}
```

This produces **one tap per hard wall, not one tap per action**. The human solves the Turnstile via the standard browser interstitial once, the agent's session cookie is established, and the agent continues. The fact that an unverified agent had to interrupt a human is logged; the audit ledger's `signed_request_skipped` counter tracks how often this happens, and the product roadmap uses that to identify which high-traffic sites to onboard into Web Bot Auth next.

---

## ⚠️ FAILURE MODES & TRUST HARDENING

### 4.1 Identity Spoofing (Mitigating the 16.2.2 Vulnerability)

**The attack:** An attacker forges a Passport document claiming to be operated by `did:web:agents.yourdomain.com:operator-jane`, signs the passport with the attacker's *own* key, and embeds the attacker's key as the `verificationMethod`. A naive verifier reads the passport, sees `proof.verificationMethod`, resolves that key, and verifies the signature — successfully — against the attacker's own public key. The attacker has impersonated the operator.

**The mitigation (16.2.2):** The verifier must **never** trust the verification method declared in the credential. It must independently resolve the issuer DID (here, `did:web:agents.yourdomain.com:operator-jane`) and use **only** the verification method found at the issuer's well-known endpoint (`https://agents.yourdomain.com/.well-known/did.json`) to verify the proof. The well-known endpoint is the only trusted source of the operator's signing key.

```typescript
// 16.2.2 mitigation — applied in the verifier
async function verifyPassport(passport: Passport): Promise<boolean> {
  // 1. Resolve the operator DID via did:web (NOT the proof's claim)
  const operatorDid = passport.issuer;  // did:web:...
  const operatorDoc = await resolver.resolve(operatorDid);
  const trustedKey = operatorDoc.didDocument
    .verificationMethod
    .find(vm => vm.id === `${operatorDid}#${expectedKeyFingerprint}`);

  // 2. Verify the proof using ONLY the operator's declared key
  const jws = passport.proof.jws;
  const canonical = jcs.canonicalize(withoutProof(passport));
  return verifyEd25519(trustedKey.publicKeyJwk, jws, canonical);
}
```

The Passport document's own `verificationMethod` field is informational only — it tells the verifier which key the operator *intends* to sign with, but the verifier cross-checks against the operator's well-known key set. Any passport that fails this cross-check is rejected, regardless of whether its internal signature verifies against its own claimed key.

### 4.2 Replay Attacks

**The attack:** An attacker captures a signed request and replays it minutes later, against a different endpoint or with a different body.

**The mitigation:** RFC 9421 supports per-request freshness. AgentGrid's signer binds:
- `created` (Unix timestamp, server time)
- `expires` (created + 60s for normal requests, +10s for state-changing requests)
- `nonce` (8 random bytes, base16)
- `@method`, `@path`, `@authority`, `content-type`, `content-digest` (body hash)

The verifier checks `expires > now > created - 300s` (small leeway for clock skew), stores seen nonces in a Redis SET with 600s TTL, and rejects any nonce collision. The 60-second window is short enough to make replay impractical and long enough to absorb normal network jitter.

For high-stakes actions (the `pay` capability, the `signup` capability), the expiry is tightened to 10 seconds and the signed components include a JCS-canonicalized hash of the full policy request envelope, making replay structurally impossible.

### 4.3 Key Compromise and Rotation

**The attack:** The agent's private signing key is exfiltrated (e.g., via prompt injection that tricks the executor into logging the key bytes — impossible by §4.1, but defend in depth).

**The mitigation:**
1. **Private key never leaves the Vault.** The Vault is a sign-only service. The signer API takes `{ did, payload }` and returns a signature; it never returns the private key bytes, even to authenticated callers. The only way to exfiltrate is to compromise the Vault itself, which lives in a separate trust zone from the LLM/agent process.
2. **Short key validity periods.** The directory entry has a `valid_until` timestamp. The Vault refuses to sign payloads whose JCS form doesn't include a `created` within the last 300 seconds — meaning a stolen Vault HMAC credential that lives for hours still cannot produce signatures more than 5 minutes old. Past that, the verifier rejects the signature as stale.
3. **Key rotation.** The agent's Passport includes a `previousKey` field if a rotation has occurred. The new key signs the rotation announcement; the old key signs a final "rotation-out" entry; the directory publishes both for a 7-day grace period. The verifier accepts signatures from either key during the grace period, then only the new key.
4. **Revocation propagation.** Setting Passport status to `revoked` in the registry causes the directory entry's `status` to flip to `revoked` within seconds (Postgres trigger → Redis pub/sub → directory edge function). The verifier checks the directory entry's status before trusting the key. For 2026 sites, Cloudflare's bot directory supports an `RTA (Revocation in the Agent)` signal — a signed revocation message cached at the edge for 5 minutes.

### 4.4 Operator-Binding Forge (16.2.1 Mitigation)

**The attack:** A compromised agent generates a fake operator key, signs a passport binding itself to that key, and issues a fully-scoped root mandate. The Mandate chain's `issuerDid` matches the passport's `operator`, but the operator is fictitious.

**The mitigation:** The Policy Engine (which authorizes the mandate before any action proceeds) checks that the root mandate's `issuerDid` matches a known operator DID, and that the operator DID resolves via `did:web` to a well-known endpoint the human controls. Without this, the system fails closed. The Passport is also subject to a separate `verifyPassport` step that resolves the operator's `did:web` URL and validates the passport's `proof` against the operator's published verification method (the 16.2.2 fix, applied here at mandate-issuance time rather than at request-verification time).

### 4.5 Site-Side Privacy and Fingerprinting

**The concern:** A signed request carries a stable agent DID, which is a stable identifier. Sites can correlate the agent's activity across visits.

**The mitigation:** This is a feature, not a bug, for accountability — but AgentGrid also supports an **ephemeral sub-key** pattern for high-privacy contexts. The agent generates a per-session Ed25519 keypair, includes the parent agent DID as a `sub` claim in a signed session token, and signs requests with the sub-key. The site can verify the session token against the parent DID without learning the parent DID's public key directly. The directory entry's `ephemeral_subkeys_allowed: true` field declares this capability.

This pattern is structurally distinct from anti-detect browser fingerprinting. The sub-key is a real cryptographic identity, not a synthetic fingerprint; the agent is still accountable (the parent DID is recoverable from the session token); the site is still protected (the parent DID is anchored to a human). It is the privacy-preserving path, not the evasion path.

### 4.6 Rate Limiting and Anomaly Detection

Independent of the signature, the Policy Engine applies per-agent, per-operator, and per-Passport rate limits:

```typescript
// Policy Engine rate-limit guard
const limits = {
  perAgent: { window: "PT1M", max: 60 },
  perOperator: { window: "PT1H", max: 5000 },
  perMandate: { window: "PT1D", max: 10000 },
  perTargetHost: { window: "PT1M", max: 30 },
};

if (this.exceedsLimit(limits, ctx)) {
  return { verdict: "DENY", reason: "rate_limit_exceeded" };
}
```

If an agent's signed-request volume spikes 10× baseline (CAEP-style continuous evaluation), the Passport is auto-suspended and a STEP_UP is triggered. Sites that detect anomalous behavior from a signed agent can submit a signed complaint to a `abuse-report` endpoint at the operator's well-known directory, and the operator's tooling can revoke or amend Mandates.

### 4.7 The Detection-Evasion Prohibition (Hard Rule)

AgentGrid's `policy_url` and `agent_brand` fields in the directory make a positive declaration:

```
"policy_url": "https://agentgrid.dev/policy/agent-conduct",
"agent_brand": "AgentGrid",
"honesty_statement": {
  "uses_captcha_solvers": false,
  "uses_anti_detect_browsers": false,
  "uses_residential_proxy_rotation": false,
  "fingerprint_spoofing": false,
  "declaration_only": true,
  "verified_by": "operator-key-2026-06-12"
}
```

Sites can use the `honesty_statement` to make policy decisions: an agent that signs its requests but also confesses to using solvers is a contradiction in terms and can be rejected at the WAF. AgentGrid-signed agents that later turn out to be lying (caught by a site audit) get their directory entry's `honesty_statement.verified_by` revoked — the operator's well-known directory stops vouching for them. This is the social/cryptographic layer that makes the whole system defensible: any AgentGrid-signed agent is one that has contractually, cryptographically, and reputationally committed to clean signature presentation.

### 4.8 Failure Mode Summary Matrix

| Failure Mode | Detection | Mitigation | Blast Radius |
|---|---|---|---|
| Signature replay | Nonce cache + expiry check | 60s window, server-side nonce store | Single request |
| Key compromise | Vault HSM + short validity | Rotation flow + 5-min signing window | Sub-minute |
| Operator binding forge (16.2.1) | Policy engine root-mandate check | DID resolution against operator's well-known | Mandate-level |
| Passport signer forge (16.2.2) | Verifier ignores proof's own key | Resolved from operator's did:web directory | Passport-level |
| Turnstile solver attempt | Honesty statement + site audit | Directory revocation | Agent-level |
| Anomalous request volume | CAEP-style detector | Auto-suspend + STEP_UP | Per-agent |
| Site doesn't honor signing | None (not a fault) | STEP_UP fallback to human | One tap per wall |
| Anti-detect browser introduced | Honesty statement check | Operator revokes | Agent-level |

### 4.9 The One Non-Negotiable

The protocol exists because the alternative — *deceiving* sites about the agent's automated nature — is structurally unsound. Detection-evasion degrades monotonically as detectors improve; signed declaration improves monotonically as more sites onboard. AgentGrid's contract with the open web is: **declare, prove, audit, and let the site decide.** Sites that welcome accountable agents get accountable agents; sites that don't, the human solves the one challenge on the wall. No solver, no proxy, no fingerprint game. The signed audit trail in the directory is the public commitment; the operator's signature is the bond.

This is the only path that scales.

---
